detection_pattern: Monitor for HTTP requests containing encoded or obfuscated path traversal sequences (%2e%2e/, ../) to COMOS web component endpoints; log entries showing unusual file access patterns outside intended directories; web application firewall alerts on POST/GET requests with code injection patterns; abnormal process execution spawned by COMOS application service; unexpected outbound connections from COMOS services; modifications to COMOS installation directories with timestamps correlating to web requests
mitigation_strategy: Immediately upgrade to COMOS V10.3.3.3 or later (verified patched versions); implement web application firewall (WAF) rules blocking path traversal attempts; disable web components if not required; restrict network access to COMOS web interfaces using network segmentation and IP whitelisting; implement input validation and sanitization for all web component parameters; monitor and log all file access attempts; apply principle of least privilege to COMOS service account; conduct regular security scanning of COMOS installations

threat_vector: Unauthorized configuration file download from SYNC2101 and related SYNC device families
exploitation_method: Attacker sends unauthenticated HTTP requests to predictable endpoints (typically /config, /backup, or /download endpoints) to retrieve the device configuration file, which may contain sensitive credentials, network settings, and security parameters
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) HTTP GET/POST requests to configuration-related endpoints from unauthenticated sessions; (2) 200 OK responses returning configuration file contents to unauthorized IPs; (3) Unusual file download activities from device management interfaces; (4) Access logs showing retrieval of .conf, .cfg, .bak, or .bin files without proper authentication headers; (5) Multiple failed authentication attempts followed by successful file retrieval
mitigation_strategy: Implement mandatory authentication before accessing configuration endpoints; require multi-factor authentication for sensitive operations; implement role-based access controls (RBAC) restricting configuration downloads; apply the latest firmware patches addressing this vulnerability; disable HTTP access and enforce HTTPS with TLS 1.2+; implement network segmentation isolating device management interfaces; monitor and log all configuration file access attempts; implement rate limiting on file download endpoints

threat_vector: Arbitrary Class Loading via Class.forName() in Apache Kylin
exploitation_method: Attacker provides malicious class name through user input parameters that are passed directly to Class.forName() without validation, allowing instantiation of arbitrary classes from the classpath, potentially triggering static initializers or constructors with malicious code
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Class.forName() calls with user-controlled input parameters in logs; (2) Unexpected class loading attempts of unusual or suspicious classes (e.g., java.lang.Runtime, ProcessBuilder wrappers); (3) HTTP requests to Kylin endpoints with suspicious 'class' or 'className' parameters containing dots and package paths; (4) ClassNotFoundException or NoClassDefFoundError errors for non-standard classes; (5) Spike in reflection-based API calls; (6) Java process spawning child processes immediately after class loading operations
mitigation_strategy: Implement input validation whitelist for allowed class names before Class.forName() calls; use custom SecurityManager to restrict ClassLoader access; apply Java deserialization filters; upgrade to patched Kylin versions (2.6.7+, 3.1.3+, or 4.x); implement network segmentation to limit Kylin service exposure; disable dynamic class loading features if not required; use parameterized APIs instead of reflection-based dynamic loading where possible

threat_vector: Memory corruption in Bluetooth controller via oversized LMP packet on 2-DH1 link causing denial of service
exploitation_method: Attacker sends malformed LMP (Link Manager Protocol) packet exceeding expected buffer size for 2-DH1 link type (max 54 bytes payload). BlueCore controller fails to validate packet length before writing to fixed memory buffer, causing heap/stack overflow and crashing the Bluetooth stack.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unexpected LMP packet size violations in Bluetooth firmware logs, (2) BlueCore controller resets/crashes following reception of malformed packets from specific peer devices, (3) Memory access violations in LMP packet handling routines, (4) Repeated connection drops from same source MAC address after oversized packet receipt, (5) Firmware watchdog timeout events correlating with unusual packet sizes
mitigation_strategy: Implement strict input validation: enforce maximum packet size limits per link type (2-DH1 = 54 bytes max) before buffer operations, add bounds checking in LMP packet parsing, deploy firmware updates with proper length validation, enable memory protection (DEP/ASLR if available), configure connection limits to mitigate fuzzing attempts, monitor and block devices sending malformed packets, maintain updated Bluetooth controller firmware

threat_vector: BookStack Improper Access Control vulnerability allowing unauthorized access to sensitive documentation
exploitation_method: Attacker bypasses role-based access control (RBAC) by manipulating URL parameters, API endpoints, or session tokens to access books, chapters, or pages they lack permission to view. Common vectors include direct object reference attacks (IDOR), privilege escalation through role manipulation, or exploiting flaws in permission inheritance logic.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: HTTP 200 responses to sensitive endpoints from unprivileged users, repeated 403→200 status code transitions on same resource IDs, API calls with modified user_id or role parameters in requests, access logs showing users retrieving content outside their assigned scope, unusual sequential ID enumeration patterns, privilege level changes in audit logs without administrative action
mitigation_strategy: Implement and enforce strict server-side access control checks on every resource access; validate user permissions against database role assignments on each request, not just client-side. Use parameterized access control: verify user belongs to correct book/chapter ownership group before serving content. Implement consistent permission inheritance validation. Apply principle of least privilege in default role assignments. Enable comprehensive audit logging of all content access attempts. Conduct regular access control testing and security code review of permission-checking logic.

threat_vector: Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) vulnerability in QTS/QuTS hero/QuTScloud allowing remote code injection
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious JavaScript payload and injects it through unvalidated input fields (URL parameters, form submissions, or API endpoints). When legitimate users access the compromised page, the script executes in their browser context with their session privileges, enabling credential theft, session hijacking, or further system compromise.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unusual HTML/JavaScript characters in request parameters (script tags, event handlers like onerror, onload); (2) POST/GET requests containing encoded payloads (%3Cscript%3E, javascript:, data:text/html); (3) Abnormal DOM modifications in browser console logs; (4) Content Security Policy (CSP) violations in security logs; (5) Unexpected outbound connections from admin interfaces; (6) Multiple requests from same user with varying suspicious parameter values
mitigation_strategy: Implement: (1) Input validation and sanitization on all user inputs using allowlists; (2) Output encoding (HTML, URL, JavaScript encoding) based on context; (3) Enforce strict Content Security Policy headers to restrict inline scripts; (4) Apply latest security patches for QTS/QuTS hero/QuTScloud immediately; (5) Use HTTPOnly and Secure flags on session cookies; (6) Deploy WAF rules to block common XSS patterns; (7) Conduct security code review of affected components; (8) Implement DOM-based XSS protections in client-side code

threat_vector: Adversary-in-the-Middle (AitM)
exploitation_method: Attacker positions themselves between two communicating parties by compromising network infrastructure (ARP spoofing, DNS hijacking, BGP hijacking), rogue wireless access points, or SSL/TLS stripping to intercept, monitor, and potentially modify traffic in transit.
detection_pattern: Certificate validation failures; unexpected certificate issuer changes; SSL/TLS cipher downgrades to weak algorithms; ARP anomalies (duplicate MAC addresses, gratuitous ARP floods); DNS query responses from unexpected sources; asymmetric network paths; sudden increases in packet retransmissions; HTTP traffic on ports typically encrypted; browser certificate warnings in logs; mismatch between expected and actual gateway MAC addresses.
mitigation_strategy: Enforce HTTPS/TLS 1.2+ with certificate pinning for critical applications; implement DNSSEC validation; deploy ARP monitoring and DHCP snooping; use mutual TLS authentication; enable Strict-Transport-Security (HSTS) headers; segment network with VLANs and access controls; monitor for certificate anomalies with YARA rules; implement network-based IDS/IPS with AitM detection signatures; enforce 802.1X authentication on network ports; use hardware security keys for critical authentication; maintain updated certificate revocation lists (CRL/OCSP).

threat_vector: Encrypted/Encoded File
exploitation_method: Attacker encrypts or encodes malicious payloads (executables, scripts, documents) to evade signature-based detection and content inspection. Common techniques include base64 encoding, XOR encryption, AES encryption, or proprietary schemes embedded in files or within archives. Payload is decrypted/decoded at runtime by dropper or loader.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unusual file entropy scores indicating compression/encryption, files with suspicious magic bytes, processes spawning child processes with encoded command-line arguments, temporary files with high randomness in hex dumps, mismatched file extensions to actual content type, multiple encoding layers detected by file analysis, suspicious use of encryption libraries or base64 functions in scripts, files written to temp directories with encrypted content followed by execution attempts.
mitigation_strategy: Deploy behavioral analysis and sandboxing to detonate suspicious files in isolated environments; use entropy analysis and file magic byte validation to identify encrypted content; implement application whitelisting to restrict execution of decoded/decrypted payloads; enforce code signing requirements; monitor for suspicious decryption/decompression routines in memory; use YARA rules targeting common encoding patterns; maintain updated threat intelligence on known malware obfuscation techniques; configure endpoint protection to block execution of files with suspicious entropy scores or suspicious process injection patterns.

threat_vector: Heap memory corruption via unvalidated HWTC IQ Capture command parameters in Snapdragon platforms
exploitation_method: Attacker sends malformed HWTC IQ Capture command with oversized or malicious input parameters that bypass input validation, causing heap buffer overflow and memory corruption, potentially leading to code execution or denial of service
detection_pattern: Monitor for: heap allocation failures, segmentation faults in HWTC driver processes, unusual memory access patterns near heap boundaries, crashdumps showing corruption in HWTC command processing buffers, abnormal IQ Capture command sizes exceeding expected parameters, repeated failed HWTC initialization attempts
mitigation_strategy: Implement strict input validation and bounds checking for all HWTC IQ Capture command parameters before processing, use safe memory allocation functions with size limits, enable heap canaries and Address Space Layout Randomization (ASLR), apply firmware patches addressing HWTC input validation, restrict HWTC command access to privileged users only, deploy memory protection techniques like Control Flow Guard (CFG), maintain memory sanitizer tools in development and testing pipelines

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Botnet (T1205.001 - Traffic Signaling)
exploitation_method: Attacker deploys malware that establishes command and control (C2) communication channels, often through HTTP/HTTPS beaconing, DNS tunneling, or P2P protocols. Infected hosts receive commands to participate in DDoS attacks, data exfiltration, or lateral movement while maintaining persistence through scheduled tasks, registry modifications, or service installations.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unusual outbound connections to known C2 domains/IPs, high volume of failed DNS queries to non-existent domains, periodic HTTP POST requests to suspicious URLs with identical patterns across multiple hosts, process creation anomalies (rundll32, powershell executing from temp directories), network beaconing at regular intervals, spike in outbound traffic volume, and successful lateral movement attempts within network segments.
mitigation_strategy: Implement network segmentation and egress filtering to restrict outbound connections to approved destinations only. Deploy DNS filtering and sinkholing of known malicious domains. Use endpoint detection and response (EDR) tools to identify and isolate infected hosts. Maintain updated antivirus/anti-malware signatures and enable behavioral detection. Enforce application whitelisting and restrict execution from temporary directories. Monitor process execution and network connections with SIEM correlation. Conduct regular vulnerability patching and disable unnecessary services. Implement MFA and credential hygiene to prevent lateral movement post-infection.

threat_vector: Double File Extension (ATT&CK T1036.007)
exploitation_method: Attacker creates files with dual extensions (e.g., document.pdf.exe, image.jpg.scr) to bypass file type validation. Windows and some email clients execute the rightmost extension while displaying the leftmost, deceiving users into opening malicious executables.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: files with executable extensions preceded by document/media extensions in logs; email gateway alerts for .exe/.scr/.bat/.cmd with spoofed document extensions; file creation events where MIME type mismatches declared extension; process execution from files with double extensions; suspicious file uploads with compound extensions in web application logs
mitigation_strategy: Implement file type validation based on file magic bytes/MIME type, not just extension; configure email gateway to block or strip dual-extension files; disable file extension hiding in Windows; use application whitelisting to prevent execution of files from user directories; train users to verify full filenames and disable 'hide known file extensions' setting; apply file association controls to prevent execution of suspicious extension combinations

threat_vector: Reflected XSS in Chaty WordPress plugin via unsanitized search parameter in admin dashboard
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious URL with JavaScript payload in search parameter (e.g., ?search=<script>alert('xss')</script>). When admin visits this link, the unsanitized search value is reflected directly in HTML output, executing attacker's JavaScript in admin context with session cookies and capabilities.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Admin dashboard access logs with search parameter containing script tags or event handlers (alert, onclick, onerror, etc.); (2) WAF rules triggering on admin.php requests with <script>, javascript:, or encoded variants in query strings; (3) Unusual JavaScript execution from admin pages in network traffic or CSP violation reports; (4) Plugin version logs showing Chaty < 2.8.3 or Chaty Pro < 2.8.2
mitigation_strategy: Immediately update Chaty to 2.8.3+ and Chaty Pro to 2.8.2+. Implement input validation/sanitization on search parameter using WordPress sanitize_text_field() and output escaping with esc_attr() or esc_html(). Deploy Content Security Policy headers to restrict inline script execution. Review admin user accounts for unauthorized activity post-incident. Consider disabling the plugin until patched if immediate updates unavailable.

threat_vector: Dnsmasq 2.86 heap-based buffer overflow in extract_name function (CVE-2021-45951 through CVE-2021-45957)
exploitation_method: Attacker sends specially crafted DNS packets with malformed domain names to trigger buffer overflow in extract_name() function when processing DNS responses or authentication data. The overflow occurs due to insufficient bounds checking on DNS name label length fields, allowing heap memory corruption.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Dnsmasq process crashes or abnormal termination; (2) segmentation faults in dnsmasq logs; (3) unusual DNS queries with exceptionally long labels or malformed name compression pointers; (4) heap corruption indicators via AddressSanitizer/valgrind if enabled; (5) unexpected dnsmasq service restarts; (6) network traffic containing DNS packets with label length fields exceeding 63 octets
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade dnsmasq to version 2.87 or later; implement network-based DNS packet validation to reject malformed queries; run dnsmasq with least privilege and memory protection (ASLR, DEP); use resource limits to contain impact of crashes; monitor dnsmasq process health with automatic restart on failure; consider running dnsmasq in a container with restricted capabilities; apply input validation at network perimeter to filter malicious DNS traffic

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Sudo and Sudo Caching
exploitation_method: Attacker with valid credentials uses sudo with password caching enabled. After initial authentication, subsequent sudo commands execute without re-authentication within the cache timeout window (default 15 minutes). Attacker escalates privileges for lateral movement or persistence without additional credential entry.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Multiple sudo commands from same user in rapid succession without password prompts in auth logs; (2) Unusual time gaps between sudo invocations matching sudo timeout settings; (3) sudo_logsrv or sudoers audit logs showing authentication timestamp anomalies; (4) Process execution chains showing privilege escalation without corresponding PAM password authentication events; (5) /var/log/auth.log entries with 'sudo: pam_unix(sudo-i:session)' without corresponding 'sudo: pam_unix(sudo:auth): password'
mitigation_strategy: Implement strict sudo configurations: (1) Set 'Defaults use_pty' to force pseudo-terminal allocation; (2) Configure 'Defaults timestamp_timeout=0' to disable password caching entirely or set minimal window; (3) Use 'Defaults:user authenticate' for per-user enforcement; (4) Implement 'Defaults log_input, log_output' for full session recording; (5) Deploy sudo version >=1.9.x with improved security controls; (6) Monitor and restrict sudoers file with 'NOPASSWD' entries; (7) Implement MFA for sudo commands; (8) Use centralized authentication (LDAP/AD) with short credential timeout settings

threat_vector: addBinding integer overflow in xmlparse.c (Expat/libexpat < 2.4.3)
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious XML with excessive namespace bindings to trigger integer overflow in addBinding function, causing heap buffer overflow and potential arbitrary code execution via malformed binding data structures
detection_pattern: Monitor for: abnormally large namespace binding counts in XML parsing, heap corruption signatures in memory dumps, crashes in libexpat with xmlparse.c stack traces, segmentation faults during XML processing of untrusted input, fuzzing-triggered failures with namespace-heavy payloads
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade libexpat to version 2.4.3 or later; implement strict XML size limits and namespace binding count validation before processing; use sandboxing for XML parsing operations; enable AddressSanitizer/MemorySanitizer in development; disable external entity processing; validate XML schema before parsing untrusted documents

threat_vector: Local privilege escalation via symlink following in Trend Micro Apex One and Worry-Free Business Security
exploitation_method: Attacker creates symbolic links in predictable temporary or cache directories that the Trend Micro service writes to with elevated privileges. When the service follows these symlinks without validation, arbitrary files can be overwritten, leading to privilege escalation or denial of service.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unusual symlink creation in %TEMP%, %ProgramData%, or Trend Micro cache directories; (2) Service process writing to unexpected file paths; (3) Failed file access attempts with privilege escalation; (4) Trend Micro service crash logs with file permission errors; (5) Rapid symlink creation/deletion patterns; (6) Changes to critical system files with Trend Micro service as originator
mitigation_strategy: Apply latest patches from Trend Micro; Implement strict file permissions on temporary and cache directories (restrict write access); Deploy Application Whitelisting to control which processes can create symlinks; Use AppLocker/Device Guard policies; Monitor and restrict local administrator access; Enable File Integrity Monitoring on Trend Micro installation directories; Configure SELinux or equivalent to prevent symlink following in service contexts; Restrict local access to machines running Trend Micro services

threat_vector: Weak password enforcement in Daybyday CRM user update functionality (versions 1.1-2.2.0)
exploitation_method: Authenticated user with password update privileges submits a weak password (e.g., single dictionary word, short string, no complexity) through the user profile update endpoint. The application accepts and stores the weak password without enforcing minimum complexity requirements (length, character diversity, entropy checks).
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Password update requests with payloads containing simple strings <8 characters or lacking character variety in access logs; (2) POST/PUT requests to user profile endpoints with weak password patterns; (3) Successful password change events followed immediately by failed authentication attempts with simple credential variants; (4) Configuration audit showing password policy rules disabled or missing in application settings; (5) Authentication logs showing successful logins with suspiciously simple credentials from user accounts.
mitigation_strategy: Implement mandatory password complexity validation: enforce minimum 12+ character length, require mixed character types (uppercase, lowercase, numbers, symbols), reject common dictionary words and previously compromised passwords, integrate NIST-compliant password validators, implement rate limiting on password change endpoints, enforce password change history to prevent reuse, add pre-change complexity warnings in UI, audit all password update events with timestamp and IP logging, and upgrade to Daybyday CRM version 2.2.1+ which includes remediated password policy enforcement.

threat_vector: Stored XSS in Daybyday CRM v2.2.0 - Task Title Field
exploitation_method: Low-privileged user submits malicious JavaScript payload in task title field (e.g., <script>alert('xss')</script>). Input is stored unsanitized in database. When any user views the task list or task details, the script executes in their browser context with their session privileges.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) HTTP POST requests to task creation endpoints containing script tags or event handlers in title parameters, (2) Database queries showing HTML/JavaScript entities in task title fields, (3) Browser console errors or unexpected script execution in task management pages, (4) Audit logs showing task modifications with suspicious character sequences: '<script', 'onerror=', 'onclick=', 'javascript:', (5) WAF alerts on outbound script execution from task detail pages
mitigation_strategy: Implement output encoding: HTML-encode all task title data on display using context-aware encoding libraries. Apply input validation: whitelist alphanumeric and safe characters in title fields, reject payloads containing angle brackets or event handlers. Use Content Security Policy (CSP) with script-src 'self' to prevent inline script execution. Upgrade to Daybyday CRM version 2.2.1+ with security patches. Conduct input/output validation testing and implement parameterized queries to prevent stored XSS persistence.

threat_vector: Unauthenticated Remote Code Execution via Cisco ASA/FTD Web Services Interface Vulnerability
exploitation_method: Attacker sends specially crafted HTTP requests to the web services interface (typically port 443) exploiting a buffer overflow or injection flaw in request parsing. The vulnerability allows bypassing authentication checks and executing arbitrary code with root privileges on the appliance.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: malformed HTTP requests to ASA/FTD web interface with unusual Content-Length headers or payload structures; POST requests to /admin or /webvpn endpoints from unauthenticated sources; unexpected process spawning from httpd/webserver processes; authentication bypass attempts in access logs showing successful requests without valid session tokens; spike in 400/500 HTTP error responses followed by successful connections; syslog entries showing unexpected command execution or privilege elevation
mitigation_strategy: Apply vendor security patches immediately; restrict access to web management interface via ACLs (limit to trusted IP ranges); disable web services if not required; implement network segmentation isolating management interfaces; deploy intrusion prevention signatures targeting this vulnerability; enable detailed logging and alerting on web interface access attempts; conduct regular vulnerability scanning; maintain offsite backups; monitor for indicators of compromise including unexpected outbound connections from appliance

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Application or System Exploitation (T1203)
exploitation_method: Attacker identifies unpatched vulnerabilities in applications or operating systems and delivers malicious payloads through crafted inputs, network packets, or malicious files. Common vectors include buffer overflows, SQL injection, command injection, or known CVEs. Exploitation gains code execution with the privileges of the vulnerable process.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unusual process spawning from application binaries, unexpected child processes with elevated privileges, segmentation faults or application crashes followed by suspicious process creation, system logs showing exploitation attempts (e.g., buffer overflow patterns), IDS/IPS alerts for known exploit signatures, abnormal application behavior (resource consumption spikes, unexpected network connections), Windows Event ID 4688 showing suspicious parent-child process relationships, application error logs indicating memory access violations or input validation failures
mitigation_strategy: Establish and enforce patch management program with regular updates for all applications and OS components; implement input validation and sanitization in applications; deploy endpoint detection and response (EDR) tools with exploit prevention capabilities; use address space layout randomization (ASLR) and data execution prevention (DEP); maintain application whitelisting; conduct regular vulnerability assessments and penetration testing; implement network segmentation to limit lateral movement post-exploitation; keep detailed inventory of software versions; configure security monitoring to alert on exploitation indicators; enable Windows Defender Exploit Guard or equivalent exploit mitigation features

threat_vector: Memory leak in Mirantis Container Runtime 20.10.8 during TLS handshakes with FIPS mode enabled, exploitable for denial of service
exploitation_method: Attacker repeatedly initiates TLS connections to the container runtime endpoint when FIPS mode is active, causing memory not to be properly deallocated after each handshake completes. Over time, accumulated memory consumption exhausts available resources, causing the runtime to become unresponsive or crash.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Steadily increasing memory consumption of containerd/docker daemon process despite stable container count, (2) Memory growth correlated with connection attempts to TLS endpoints (ports 2376, 2377 depending on configuration), (3) High number of completed TLS sessions in netstat/ss output, (4) containerd logs showing repeated TLS handshake operations, (5) Out-of-memory kernel messages targeting container runtime process, (6) Performance degradation and increased swap usage over time
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade Mirantis Container Runtime to version 20.10.9 or later where the memory leak is patched. As interim mitigation: (1) Disable FIPS mode if operationally feasible, (2) Implement connection rate limiting on TLS endpoints using firewall/ingress rules, (3) Set memory limits on container runtime using cgroups/systemd, (4) Configure automatic restart of container runtime service on high memory threshold, (5) Monitor and alert on runtime process memory utilization, (6) Restrict TLS endpoint access to trusted networks only

threat_vector: ATT&CK: T1574.001 - DLL Search Order Hijacking
exploitation_method: Attacker places malicious DLL in search path (current directory, System32, or application directory) with same name as legitimate DLL. When application calls LoadLibrary without full path, Windows loads attacker's DLL first, executing arbitrary code with application privileges.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: DLL loads from unexpected directories, DLL files in user-writable locations with system DLL names, API calls to LoadLibrary with relative paths, unsigned DLLs in system directories, file creation events in application directories prior to application launch, parent-child process relationships where child loads suspicious DLL
mitigation_strategy: Configure applications to use full paths for DLL loading, enable DLL Safe Search ordering via registry (HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Control\Session Manager\SafeDllSearchMode = 1), use AppLocker/Windows Defender Application Control to whitelist legitimate DLLs, regularly audit DLL load events, remove unnecessary DLLs from application directories, implement code signing requirements, keep file permissions restrictive on system directories

threat_vector: Improper access control allowing authenticated users to access unauthorized sensitive data
exploitation_method: Authenticated attacker manipulates request parameters (object IDs, user IDs, file paths) or API endpoints to directly reference resources they lack permission to access, bypassing authorization checks. Common techniques include parameter tampering, insecure direct object references (IDOR), or exploiting missing role-based access control (RBAC) validation.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Sequential or random parameter enumeration attempts (e.g., user_id=1,2,3...); (2) Access log entries showing authenticated users requesting resources outside their permission scope; (3) Unusual cross-tenant or cross-department data access patterns; (4) 200 OK responses for resource requests that should return 403 Forbidden; (5) Same user session accessing disparate sensitive data types within short timeframes; (6) API calls with modified object/resource identifiers returning data instead of access denied errors.
mitigation_strategy: Implement server-side authorization checks on every data access: (1) Verify user permissions against requested resource before returning data, not just at UI layer; (2) Use indirect references and session-based lookups instead of exposing direct object IDs; (3) Enforce role-based access control (RBAC) with principle of least privilege; (4) Log all data access attempts with user, resource, and timestamp; (5) Conduct access control testing including IDOR and privilege escalation scenarios; (6) Implement rate limiting on resource enumeration; (7) Use allowlists of permissible resources per user role.

threat_vector: Configuration defects in Smartphone MEID/IMEI permission escalation
exploitation_method: Attacker exploits misconfigured device settings or firmware vulnerabilities that fail to properly restrict access to MEID/IMEI identifiers. By leveraging debug interfaces, exploiting SELinux policy weaknesses, or using privileged APIs without proper permission checks, the attacker gains unauthorized access to device identity information and potentially modifies device identifiers.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unusual system calls accessing /dev/radio or modem interfaces without proper SELinux context; (2) Unexpected read/write access to baseband firmware or NVRAM partitions; (3) Processes calling getDeviceId() or related telephony APIs with unexpected UIDs; (4) SELinux denials related to 'radio' or 'system' domain accessing restricted telephony attributes; (5) Unexpected modification timestamps on /efs or /persist partitions; (6) ADB commands executed with shell privilege accessing telephony configuration; (7) Logcat entries showing permission bypass attempts in telephony service.
mitigation_strategy: Implement: (1) Strict SELinux policy enforcement preventing non-authorized processes from accessing MEID/IMEI storage; (2) Regular firmware security audits and patching for configuration vulnerabilities; (3) Restrict debug interface access (ADB, UART) with strong authentication; (4) Implement proper permission checks in telephony service APIs; (5) Use verified boot and code signing to prevent unauthorized firmware modification; (6) Enable runtime application permission denial for sensitive telephony APIs; (7) Deploy Mobile Device Management (MDM) to enforce secure device configuration baselines; (8) Monitor and alert on partition modification attempts.

threat_vector: Default credentials on admin interface
exploitation_method: Attacker accesses publicly documented default username/password combinations (e.g., admin/admin, administrator/password) to authenticate to management consoles, control panels, or administrative web interfaces without authorization.
detection_pattern: Successful login attempts from unusual IP addresses or geographic locations using default account names; repeated failed authentication attempts followed by successful login; admin account logins outside normal business hours; access to admin interface from external/untrusted networks; audit logs showing first-time admin access without corresponding provisioning tickets.
mitigation_strategy: Force password change on first login; disable or rename default accounts; implement mandatory strong password policies for administrative accounts; restrict admin interface access by IP whitelist/VPN; enable multi-factor authentication for all administrative access; monitor and alert on any default credential usage; conduct regular audits to identify remaining default accounts; automate compliance scanning to detect exposed default credentials.

threat_vector: Integer overflow in xmlparse.c lookup function in Expat library versions before 2.4.3
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious XML documents with specially crafted entity definitions or namespace declarations that trigger integer overflow in the lookup table size calculation, leading to buffer overflow or heap corruption when the parser processes the data
detection_pattern: Monitor for: Expat library version < 2.4.3 in use; XML parsing crashes with SIGABRT or SIGSEGV; heap sanitizer alerts on heap-buffer-overflow during XML parsing; abnormally large entity declarations or namespace definitions in XML input; memory access violations in xmlparse.c during XML document processing
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade Expat library to version 2.4.3 or later immediately; implement input validation to reject XML documents with excessively large entity or namespace declarations; run applications with memory protection tools (ASLR, stack canaries, heap hardening); implement XML schema validation to constrain document structure before parsing; consider sandboxing XML processing operations

threat_vector: Soketi WebSocket server POST request body read failure causing denial of service
exploitation_method: Attacker sends malformed POST requests with incomplete or unreadable body content, triggering an unhandled exception in the request parsing logic that crashes the Soketi server process
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Sudden increase in failed POST requests with 4xx/5xx errors, (2) Server process crashes correlated with incoming POST requests, (3) Log entries showing unhandled exceptions during request body parsing, (4) Connection resets immediately after POST request submission, (5) System logs indicating unexpected process termination of Soketi worker threads
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade Soketi to patched version handling malformed POST bodies gracefully; implement request body size limits; add try-catch wrappers around body parsing logic; deploy rate limiting on POST endpoints; configure process monitoring (systemd/supervisord) to auto-restart crashed instances; place reverse proxy (nginx) with request validation in front of Soketi to filter malformed requests before they reach the server

threat_vector: CSCMS v4.1 SQL injection via page_del function
exploitation_method: Attacker sends malicious SQL syntax in the page_del parameter without proper input validation or parameterized queries, allowing arbitrary SQL commands to execute against the backend database
detection_pattern: Monitor for: SQL metacharacters (', ", --, /*, ;) in page_del parameter logs; database error messages in application logs; unusual database queries; unexpected database connection activity; audit logs showing DELETE/SELECT operations from the page_del handler outside normal patterns
mitigation_strategy: Implement parameterized prepared statements for all database queries in page_del function; apply strict input validation (whitelist numeric IDs only); use ORM frameworks that handle query escaping; apply principle of least privilege to database user account; implement Web Application Firewall (WAF) rules to block SQL injection patterns; conduct security code review of page_del function; update CSCMS to patched version if available

threat_vector: Insecure storage of sensitive information in Ivanti Workspace Control <2021.2 (10.7.30.0) allowing local privilege escalation through plaintext credential extraction
exploitation_method: Attacker with local low-privilege access reads unencrypted sensitive data (cryptographic keys, credentials) from Ivanti Workspace Control configuration files or registry hives stored in world-readable locations or insufficiently protected memory/disk locations
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unusual file access attempts to Ivanti config directories by low-privilege accounts, (2) Registry access patterns targeting HKLM\Software\Ivanti paths, (3) Process dumps or memory forensics of Workspace Control services, (4) Unauthorized reads of files in Program Files\Ivanti\Workspace Control or user AppData paths containing .config, .key, or .xml files, (5) Elevation of privilege events following Workspace Control installation
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade Ivanti Workspace Control to version 2021.2 (10.7.30.0) or later; implement file system ACLs restricting read access to Workspace Control configuration directories to SYSTEM and administrators only; enable Windows DPAPI encryption for stored credentials; deploy application whitelisting to prevent unauthorized credential extraction tools; conduct regular audits of file permissions in Ivanti installation directories; implement privileged access management (PAM) solutions to monitor and restrict local administrative access

threat_vector: Telephony application exposes sensitive information to unauthorized actors
exploitation_method: Attacker gains unauthorized access to telephony application APIs, databases, or memory to retrieve sensitive data such as call logs, voicemails, user credentials, authentication tokens, or personal information without proper authorization checks
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unusual API requests with missing/invalid authorization headers, database queries from non-authenticated sources, access to sensitive endpoints without proper credentials, spike in failed authentication attempts followed by successful access, data exfiltration patterns (large data transfers to external IPs), application logs showing access to sensitive data fields without corresponding user actions, error messages revealing system information or data structure details
mitigation_strategy: Implement strict access controls with role-based authorization (RBAC), encrypt sensitive data at rest and in transit (TLS 1.2+), validate all API requests for proper authentication and authorization before returning any data, implement rate limiting and anomaly detection on API endpoints, sanitize error messages to prevent information disclosure, conduct regular security audits and penetration testing, use secrets management systems for credentials, implement comprehensive audit logging of all sensitive data access, apply principle of least privilege to all application accounts

threat_vector: WebSocket hijacking
exploitation_method: Attacker intercepts WebSocket handshake or session, exploiting missing origin validation, lack of CSRF tokens, or weak authentication. Attacker can then inject malicious messages, eavesdrop on communications, or impersonate legitimate clients by reusing session identifiers or hijacking unencrypted WS connections.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unexpected WebSocket connections from non-whitelisted origins, abnormal message patterns (high frequency, unusual payload sizes), connection attempts with missing or invalid Origin/Referer headers, session tokens appearing in multiple concurrent connections from different IPs, WebSocket frame inspection showing injected commands inconsistent with user behavior, TLS certificate mismatches on WSS connections, rapid connection/disconnection cycles.
mitigation_strategy: Enforce strict origin validation on WebSocket handshake, require CSRF tokens in handshake request, use WSS (WebSocket Secure) with TLS encryption only, implement short-lived session tokens with re-authentication, validate all incoming WebSocket messages server-side, use same-origin policy strictly, implement rate limiting on WebSocket connections per IP/user, monitor and log all WebSocket handshakes and state changes, employ mutual TLS authentication where feasible, ensure proper session binding with secure cookies (Secure, HttpOnly, SameSite flags).

threat_vector: Account enumeration via timing difference
exploitation_method: Attacker submits authentication requests with varying usernames and measures response times. Valid accounts typically return different response times (longer due to password hashing) compared to invalid accounts (faster rejection), allowing enumeration of valid usernames without credentials.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) High volume of failed authentication attempts from single source targeting different usernames; (2) Statistical clustering of response times grouped by username validity; (3) Repeated login attempts with systematic username variations in short timeframes; (4) Authentication logs showing pattern of attempts before typical user login patterns; (5) Rate of failed attempts exceeding normal user behavior (>5 failures per minute per source).
mitigation_strategy: Implement constant-time authentication responses by: (1) Always performing full password hashing regardless of account existence; (2) Adding random jitter (50-200ms) to all authentication response times; (3) Rate limiting authentication attempts (max 5 per minute per IP); (4) Implementing account lockout after failed attempts; (5) Using generic error messages for both invalid user and invalid password; (6) Adding CAPTCHA after 3 failed attempts; (7) Monitoring and alerting on enumeration patterns.

threat_vector: PendingIntent Hijacking in Bixby Routines (CVE-style vulnerability affecting Android Q and R versions)
exploitation_method: Attacker creates malicious app with elevated permissions to intercept and modify PendingIntent objects in Bixby Routines. The vulnerability allows hijacking of pending intents that execute with system or routine privileges, enabling unauthorized execution of sensitive actions like device configuration changes, data access, or command execution.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unexpected PendingIntent modifications in system logs; (2) Bixby Routines execution with mismatched caller UID/GID; (3) Privilege escalation attempts in SELinux/AppArmor denials; (4) Routine executions originating from non-Bixby package contexts; (5) Suspicious intent flags (FLAG_IMMUTABLE absence in older versions); (6) Anomalous system capability invocations from third-party apps
mitigation_strategy: Immediate actions: (1) Update Bixby to version 3.1.21.8+ (Android R) or 2.6.30.5+ (Android Q); (2) Enforce FLAG_IMMUTABLE on all PendingIntent creation in Routines code; (3) Implement strict signature verification for routine execution; (4) Apply principle of least privilege to Bixby runtime permissions; (5) Deploy SELinux policy to restrict cross-app PendingIntent manipulation; (6) Monitor and audit routine modifications in real-time; (7) Disable Bixby Routines if immediate patching is unavailable

threat_vector: Insyde InsydeH2O SdHostDriver Kernel Vulnerability (CVE-2021-28656) - Uncontrolled memory access in SD host controller driver affecting kernel versions 5.1-5.5
exploitation_method: Attacker with local system access exploits improper input validation in SdHostDriver to perform arbitrary read/write operations on kernel memory via crafted IOCTL requests, potentially achieving privilege escalation or code execution
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unusual IOCTL calls to SdHostDriver with abnormal parameter sizes, unexpected kernel memory access patterns, failed memory validation errors in driver logs, process execution with elevated privileges following SD device interaction, kernel panic or system instability after specific driver API calls, audit logs showing unauthorized memory region access attempts
mitigation_strategy: Immediately patch InsydeH2O firmware to versions 05.16.25 (5.1), 05.26.25 (5.2), 05.35.25 (5.3), 05.43.25 (5.4), or 05.51.25 (5.5) or newer; restrict local system access and implement principle of least privilege; disable unnecessary SD host controller functionality if not required; monitor and restrict IOCTL access to SdHostDriver through application whitelisting; enable kernel address space layout randomization (KASLR) and Code Integrity protection; maintain offline backup firmware images and implement secure boot verification

threat_vector: Integer overflow in m_groupSize variable within doProlog function of xmlparse.c in Expat versions before 2.4.3
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious XML document with deeply nested elements or large entity declarations that causes m_groupSize counter to overflow, potentially leading to heap buffer overflow, memory corruption, or code execution when parsing with vulnerable Expat library
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) XML parsing errors followed by segmentation faults or abnormal process termination; (2) Unexpected memory access violations in libexpat during XML document processing; (3) Abnormally large or deeply nested XML structures in network traffic or file uploads; (4) Application crashes specifically during XML prolog parsing phase; (5) Memory sanitizer alerts showing heap-buffer-overflow in doProlog or related functions
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade Expat library to version 2.4.3 or later immediately; implement input validation to reject XML documents exceeding reasonable nesting depth (e.g., >100 levels); deploy XML parsers with overflow protections or use alternative parsers with better integer bounds checking; implement resource limits on XML parsing (max file size, parsing timeout); run XML processing in sandboxed environments with memory protections enabled (ASLR, DEP)

threat_vector: Heap-based buffer overflow in NFC module leading to memory corruption
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious NFC packets with oversized payloads that exceed heap buffer boundaries in the NFC driver/service, causing adjacent memory structures to be overwritten. This can lead to arbitrary code execution, privilege escalation, or denial of service.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) NFC service crashes or unexpected terminations in system logs; (2) abnormal heap memory allocation patterns or fragmentation spikes; (3) NFC packets with payload sizes exceeding protocol specifications; (4) memory access violations (segmentation faults) involving NFC driver memory regions; (5) unexpected process privilege escalation following NFC interaction; (6) Address Space Layout Randomization (ASLR) bypass attempts correlating with NFC activity.
mitigation_strategy: Implement: (1) strict input validation and bounds checking on all NFC packet payloads with maximum size enforcement; (2) use of safe memory allocation functions and heap protection mechanisms (e.g., AddressSanitizer); (3) regular security patches and updates for NFC drivers; (4) sandboxing of NFC service with minimal privileges; (5) enable compiler-level protections (stack canaries, DEP/NX bit, ASLR); (6) fuzz testing of NFC input handlers during development; (7) disable NFC when not in use.

threat_vector: Domain Fronting (ATT&CK T1090.004)
exploitation_method: Attacker uses a legitimate CDN or reverse proxy to obscure C2 traffic by placing malicious domain in SNI/Host header while connecting to provider's IP. Traffic appears to originate from trusted domain while routing to attacker-controlled backend.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: mismatches between SNI hostname and HTTP Host header, SNI values not matching destination IP geolocation, repeated connections to CDN edge IPs with rotating SNI values, TLS certificates issued to major CDN providers but hosting suspicious content, anomalous traffic patterns to known CDN provider IPs with non-standard ports
mitigation_strategy: Implement strict validation of SNI/Host header consistency; use certificate pinning for critical services; deploy DNS sinkhole for known malicious domains; configure WAF rules to block mismatched SNI/Host headers; monitor CDN provider logs for unusual backend routing; restrict outbound TLS connections to known legitimate destinations; use network segmentation to limit C2 callback destinations

threat_vector: Integer overflow in nextScaffoldPart function of xmlparse.c in Expat library versions before 2.4.3
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malformed XML with deeply nested elements or extremely large structural scaffolding data that causes integer overflow in buffer size calculations within nextScaffoldPart, leading to heap buffer overflow and arbitrary code execution
detection_pattern: Monitor for: Expat library version < 2.4.3 in use, segmentation faults during XML parsing, heap memory corruption errors, unexpected process termination during XML document processing, memory sanitizer alerts (ASAN/MSAN) showing heap-buffer-overflow in xmlparse.c:nextScaffoldPart, core dumps with stack traces pointing to XML parsing functions
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade Expat library to version 2.4.3 or later immediately; implement input validation to reject XML documents with excessive nesting depth before passing to parser; use memory-safe XML parsing alternatives; deploy application in sandboxed environment with memory protection (DEP/ASLR); monitor process execution for abnormal termination; maintain inventory of Expat usage across codebase and dependencies

threat_vector: ATT&CK: PowerShell Profile (T1546.013)
exploitation_method: Attacker modifies PowerShell profile files ($PROFILE, profile.ps1) to execute arbitrary code automatically when PowerShell starts. Common locations include CurrentUserAllHosts, CurrentUserCurrentHost, AllUsersAllHosts, AllUsersCurrentHost. Malicious commands persist and execute with user privileges on every new PowerShell session.
detection_pattern: Monitor file system for modifications to: %APPDATA%\Microsoft\Windows\PowerShell\profile.ps1, %APPDATA%\Microsoft\Windows\PowerShell\Microsoft.PowerShell_profile.ps1, %WINDIR%\System32\WindowsPowerShell\v1.0\profile.ps1. Track Process Creation events (Event ID 4688) with parent process powershell.exe containing suspicious command parameters. Monitor PowerShell ScriptBlockLogging (Event ID 4104) for profile loading with encoded/obfuscated content. Alert on unexpected registry modifications to HKCU\Software\Microsoft\PowerShell\1\ShellIds.
mitigation_strategy: Enforce PowerShell Constrained Language Mode and ExecutionPolicy via Group Policy (Set-ExecutionPolicy -ExecutionPolicy AllSigned). Restrict write permissions on profile directories using NTFS ACLs. Disable PowerShell 2.0 engine. Enable ScriptBlockLogging and Transcription logging via Group Policy. Implement AppLocker/WDAC policies to block unsigned scripts. Monitor and baseline profile files with File Integrity Monitoring (FIM) tools. Educate users not to modify profiles and regularly audit profile contents.

threat_vector: Exposed bot token in source code repository (PuddingBot main.py)
exploitation_method: Attacker discovers publicly accessible repository, locates hardcoded bot token in main.py, uses token to authenticate as the bot and perform unauthorized actions including message manipulation, user impersonation, and server takeover through the bot's permissions
detection_pattern: Search version control history and public repositories for credential patterns matching bot token format; monitor for unauthorized bot API calls from unknown IP addresses; alert on bot permission changes or suspicious command execution; scan source code commits for strings matching authentication token regex patterns (typically alphanumeric strings >20 chars in authentication contexts)
mitigation_strategy: Immediately revoke compromised token and issue new one; implement pre-commit hooks to prevent credential commits using tools like TruffleHog or git-secrets; move all tokens to environment variables or secure vaults (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault); enable bot token rotation policies; audit all bot activity logs during exposure window; use code scanning in CI/CD pipeline; implement branch protection requiring code review before merge; educate developers on credential management best practices

threat_vector: Stack-based buffer overflow in SonicOS SessionID HTTP response header
exploitation_method: Authenticated attacker sends HTTP request with excessively long SessionID parameter in response header, overwriting stack memory and corrupting return pointers or saved registers, leading to crash or arbitrary code execution
detection_pattern: Monitor for: abnormally long SessionID header values (>256 bytes), multiple 400/500 HTTP errors from same source after SessionID requests, firewall process crashes or unexpected restarts correlating with HTTP traffic spikes, stack trace logs containing buffer overflow signatures, IDS alerts on malformed header lengths, abnormal control flow in firewall processes detected via EDR
mitigation_strategy: 1) Apply SonicWall firmware patches addressing SessionID buffer bounds validation; 2) Implement input validation limiting SessionID header to defined maximum length; 3) Enable Web Application Firewall (WAF) rules rejecting oversized headers; 4) Restrict authenticated access via network segmentation; 5) Deploy stack canaries and ASLR at OS level; 6) Monitor SessionID parameter sizes and alert on anomalies; 7) Implement rate limiting on HTTP requests from authenticated users; 8) Enable code execution prevention (DEP/NX) on firewall; 9) Regular security patching and vulnerability scanning

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1565.003 - Runtime Data Manipulation
exploitation_method: Attacker injects malicious code or modifies data structures in memory during program execution, altering application behavior without touching disk artifacts. Common methods include DLL injection, process hollowing, or direct memory manipulation via debuggers/tools to change variables, function pointers, or configuration values at runtime.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unexpected process memory modifications, suspicious debugger attachment (ProcDump, WinDbg), memory anomalies via EDR tools, code caves in legitimate processes, unsigned DLLs loaded into trusted applications, abnormal API call sequences (VirtualAllocEx, WriteProcessMemory, SetWindowsHookEx), runtime integrity check failures, discrepancies between disk and memory image of executable sections.
mitigation_strategy: Deploy Code Integrity: enable ASLR and DEP/NX bits; implement Control Flow Guard (CFG) and Arbitrary Code Guard (ACG); use signed executables and enforce code signing verification; deploy runtime application self-protection (RASP); restrict debugger access via WinRM policies; monitor and alert on process memory access from suspicious tools; use kernel-mode code integrity verification; implement Data Execution Prevention at OS level; segment processes with mandatory integrity levels.

threat_vector: Unrestricted File Copy Vulnerability in /UserSelfServiceSettings.jsp (SysAid ITIL 20.4.74 b10)
exploitation_method: Authenticated attacker crafts HTTP requests to /UserSelfServiceSettings.jsp with malicious file path parameters, leveraging insufficient input validation to copy arbitrary files from the server filesystem to web-accessible directories, enabling information disclosure or further compromise
detection_pattern: Monitor for: HTTP POST/GET requests to /UserSelfServiceSettings.jsp with suspicious file path parameters (../, absolute paths, or encoded traversal sequences); unusual file copy operations in application logs; unexpected files appearing in web root directories; access attempts combining authentication tokens with file operation parameters; FileInputStream/FileOutputStream calls with user-supplied paths in application logs
mitigation_strategy: Implement strict input validation on all file path parameters with whitelist-based path constraints; enforce principle of least privilege on file system permissions; disable file copy operations in /UserSelfServiceSettings.jsp unless absolutely required; apply path canonicalization and verify resolved paths are within allowed directories; upgrade to patched SysAid version; implement Web Application Firewall rules blocking directory traversal sequences in this endpoint; conduct code review of file handling functions; restrict web server write permissions to essential directories only

threat_vector: TLR-2005KSH arbitrary file upload via enabled PUT method
exploitation_method: Attacker sends HTTP PUT request to upload malicious files (HTML, CGI, PHP, JSP) to web root directory. Since access controls are not properly enforced, files bypass validation and execute with web server privileges, enabling remote code execution or website defacement.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: HTTP PUT requests in access logs, unexpected file creation in web directories with executable extensions (.cgi, .php, .jsp, .exe), web server error logs showing 'PUT' method attempts, files with modification timestamps matching suspicious PUT requests, outbound connections from web server process, unusual process spawning from web server user account
mitigation_strategy: Disable HTTP PUT method in web server configuration (remove from allowed methods in Apache/Nginx config); implement strict input validation and file type whitelist; enforce proper file permissions (web directory not writable by web process); apply authentication and authorization checks before any file operations; isolate web root from executable directories; deploy Web Application Firewall (WAF) rules to block PUT requests; update device firmware if available; restrict file upload to designated non-executable directory; implement file integrity monitoring on web directories

threat_vector: Acquire Infrastructure - Attacker obtains or compromises infrastructure (domains, hosting, servers) for use in attacks
exploitation_method: Attacker registers domains with privacy protection, leases cloud instances, compromises legitimate servers, or purchases bulletproof hosting to establish command and control infrastructure, phishing sites, or malware distribution points
detection_pattern: Monitor for newly registered domains with similar names to legitimate targets, unusual WHOIS registration patterns, domain name system (DNS) queries to newly created domains, anomalous outbound connections to suspicious hosting providers, SSL certificate issuance for lookalike domains, rapid creation of multiple domains by single registrant
mitigation_strategy: Implement domain monitoring and threat intelligence feeds for brand-related domains, enforce DNSSEC validation, monitor Certificate Transparency logs for suspicious certificates, maintain updated DNS firewall rules, conduct regular brand protection audits, establish relationships with domain registrars for rapid takedown procedures, block known bulletproof hosting ASNs at network perimeter, and monitor employee/user access to suspicious infrastructure

threat_vector: SQL Injection in USOC CMS usersearch.php via unsanitized user search parameters
exploitation_method: Attacker submits SQL metacharacters (e.g., ' OR '1'='1) in search form fields that are directly concatenated into SQL queries without parameterization, allowing arbitrary SQL execution to extract, modify, or delete database records
detection_pattern: Monitor for SQL metacharacters in HTTP request parameters to usersearch.php (quotes, dashes, semicolons, UNION/SELECT keywords); log unusual database queries with boolean logic operators; alert on unexpected result set sizes from search queries; track failed authentication attempts following search requests
mitigation_strategy: Implement prepared statements with parameterized queries for all database operations in usersearch.php; apply input validation using whitelist-based filtering on search terms; use ORM frameworks that abstract SQL construction; deploy Web Application Firewall (WAF) rules blocking SQL injection patterns; conduct code review of all user-input handling; apply principle of least privilege to database user accounts

threat_vector: GLPI Addressing Plugin RCE (CVE-2024-28898)
exploitation_method: Authenticated attacker with valid GLPI credentials exploits unsafe deserialization or code injection in the addressing plugin versions below 2.9.1, allowing arbitrary PHP code execution on the server with GLPI application privileges
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) POST requests to /plugins/addressing/ endpoints with suspicious serialized PHP objects or eval() patterns in request parameters, (2) Unusual process spawning from PHP-FPM or Apache processes, (3) Web server error logs showing PHP fatal errors related to addressing plugin, (4) Unexpected file creation/modification in /plugins/addressing/ directory, (5) Outbound connections from GLPI process to external IPs, (6) Plugin version detection showing addressing plugin < 2.9.1
mitigation_strategy: Immediately update GLPI addressing plugin to version 2.9.1 or later; disable the addressing plugin if upgrade is unavailable; implement application-level input validation and WAF rules to block serialized PHP objects in addressing plugin endpoints; restrict GLPI user privileges to minimum required roles; enable PHP disable_functions directive to restrict dangerous functions like eval(), exec(), system(); maintain regular backups; monitor GLPI audit logs for suspicious authenticated sessions

threat_vector: Shopware Open Redirect Vulnerability in Router URL Handling
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts a malicious URL containing an unvalidated redirect parameter that bypasses Shopware's URL validation logic. The router fails to properly sanitize or validate destination URLs before performing HTTP redirects, allowing attackers to redirect users to external malicious sites while appearing to originate from the legitimate Shopware domain.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: HTTP 301/302/307 response codes with Location headers pointing to external domains; query parameters containing 'redirect', 'return', 'url', or 'goto' with external domain values in logs; unusual referrer mismatches between request origin and redirect destination; access logs showing requests to router endpoints with suspicious URL-encoded parameters; WAF alerts on incomplete or malformed URL schemes in Location headers.
mitigation_strategy: Implement strict allowlist-based URL validation before redirects - only permit redirects to known internal paths or explicitly whitelisted domains; use Shopware's built-in URL validation utilities and ensure they are current; enforce relative URL redirects only when possible; add X-Content-Type-Options and Content-Security-Policy headers to prevent exploitation; apply latest Shopware security patches immediately; conduct code review of all custom router extensions; implement detection rules for suspicious redirect parameters in WAF/IDS; educate users to verify URLs before following links in emails.

threat_vector: ATT&CK: T1071.004 - Application Layer Protocol: DNS
exploitation_method: Attacker uses DNS queries and responses as a covert communication channel to exfiltrate data or receive command-and-control instructions. DNS tunneling encapsulates arbitrary traffic within DNS protocol to bypass network filters that allow DNS but block other protocols.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unusually high DNS query volume from single host, DNS queries to non-existent domains, excessive subdomain queries, anomalously large DNS response packets, queries with suspicious patterns (random character prefixes), DNS traffic at odd hours, connections to newly registered or suspicious domains, queries with abnormal TTL values, and DNS queries originating from unexpected internal hosts or during non-business hours
mitigation_strategy: Implement DNS filtering and monitoring: deploy DNS sinkhole/blocklist for known malicious domains, enforce DNS query logging with SIEM integration, use DNS rate limiting per client, implement DNSSEC validation, restrict DNS recursion to authorized clients only, disable DNS over HTTPS/TLS if not required to maintain visibility, establish baseline of normal DNS patterns for anomaly detection, segment network to limit which hosts can perform DNS queries, and require DNS proxy/inspection for all outbound DNS traffic

threat_vector: IPv6 packet with invalid length in destination options header causing assertion in Snapdragon platforms
exploitation_method: Attacker sends crafted IPv6 packets with malformed destination options header (incorrect length field) that bypasses validation checks, triggering assertion failure in kernel or network stack processing
detection_pattern: Monitor for: IPv6 packets with destination options header length mismatches; kernel assertions or panics with stack traces referencing IPv6 header parsing functions; netstat anomalies showing connection drops; syslog entries containing 'IPv6' and 'assertion' or 'panic'; abnormal ICMPv6 error responses; unexpected device reboots correlating with IPv6 traffic spikes
mitigation_strategy: Apply Snapdragon firmware/kernel patches validating IPv6 destination options header length before processing; implement packet filter rules dropping IPv6 packets with invalid extension header lengths; deploy network segmentation restricting IPv6 from untrusted sources; enable kernel panic logging to detect exploitation attempts; maintain current security updates for affected Snapdragon chipsets

threat_vector: COMOS Web Components Remote Code Execution (CVE-2024-XXXXX)
exploitation_method: Attacker sends maliciously crafted HTTP requests to vulnerable COMOS web component endpoints, exploiting improper input validation or insecure deserialization in versions V10.2, V10.3 (<V10.3.3.3), and V10.4 (<current patched version). This allows unauthenticated or low-privilege remote code execution on the COMOS server.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unusual HTTP POST/GET requests to /comos/web or similar component paths with large/binary payloads; (2) Error logs showing deserialization exceptions or XML/serialization parsing errors; (3) Processes spawned from COMOS web service with suspicious parameters; (4) Network connections from COMOS processes to unusual external IPs; (5) File modifications in COMOS installation directories outside maintenance windows; (6) Authentication logs showing failed requests followed by successful command execution
mitigation_strategy: Immediately: (1) Upgrade to patched versions (V10.3.3.3+ or latest V10.4); (2) Disable web components if not required; (3) Implement network segmentation restricting COMOS server access to trusted IPs; (4) Deploy WAF rules blocking suspicious payloads to COMOS endpoints; (5) Enable comprehensive audit logging; (6) Run COMOS service with minimal privileges (non-root/non-admin); (7) Conduct vulnerability scanning; (8) Review recent access logs for indicators of compromise

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Hide Artifacts (T1564)
exploitation_method: Attacker modifies file attributes (hidden flag, alternate data streams), manipulates timestamps, clears logs, or uses steganography to conceal malicious files, command history, or evidence of compromise from detection systems and forensic analysis
detection_pattern: Monitor for: file attribute changes via attrib.exe or SetFileAttributes API calls; suspicious alternate data stream creation (dir /s /r); LOG_FILE_RENAMED or file modification timestamps inconsistent with access patterns; cleared Security/System/Application event logs with gaps in event IDs; creation of hidden directories (.hidden in Linux); unusual use of file system APIs for metadata manipulation
mitigation_strategy: Enable immutable logging with append-only storage and remote syslog forwarding; deploy file integrity monitoring (FIM) to detect attribute changes; enforce filesystem auditing (Windows: audit object access); restrict access to log deletion utilities; use NTFS permissions to prevent unauthorized file attribute modifications; monitor PowerShell/command-line history; implement behavioral analytics to detect log clearing patterns

threat_vector: SQL Injection vulnerability in telephony application
exploitation_method: Attacker injects malicious SQL commands through user input fields (phone numbers, customer IDs, search parameters, etc.) that are not properly sanitized. Database queries concatenate unsanitized input, allowing attackers to extract sensitive data, modify records, or execute arbitrary database operations.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: SQL syntax keywords in application logs (UNION, SELECT, DROP, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, OR, AND, --); unusual database query patterns; multiple failed authentication attempts; unexpected database connection spikes; error messages containing SQL syntax; access to tables outside normal application workflow; exfiltration of large result sets; abnormal query execution times; application error logs showing database exceptions with user-supplied data in stack traces
mitigation_strategy: Implement parameterized queries/prepared statements throughout the application; use object-relational mapping (ORM) frameworks that prevent SQL injection; apply input validation with whitelist approach for all user inputs; enforce least privilege database accounts with minimal required permissions; implement Web Application Firewall (WAF) rules to detect SQL injection patterns; conduct static code analysis and SAST scanning; perform regular security testing including penetration testing; implement database activity monitoring; apply security patches promptly; educate developers on secure coding practices

threat_vector: Chang Lian application caller ID spoofing vulnerability enabling anonymous call masking
exploitation_method: Attacker exploits insufficient input validation in Chang Lian's call routing mechanism to manipulate or suppress caller identification data before transmission to call termination points, resulting in calls displaying hidden, spoofed, or generic caller IDs
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) calls with null/empty caller ID fields in CDR logs; (2) calls from Chang Lian app with caller ID mismatches between application logs and telecom gateway records; (3) unusual patterns of calls with identical spoofed IDs from different users; (4) VoIP protocol anomalies such as missing P-Asserted-Identity or From headers; (5) rapid successive calls with varying caller ID obfuscation attempts; (6) application traffic to unauthorized caller ID modification endpoints
mitigation_strategy: Implement: (1) application-level input validation and sanitization for all caller ID parameters; (2) mandatory caller ID verification against registered phone numbers before call initiation; (3) network-level enforcement of authenticated calling line identification presentation (CLIP) standards; (4) API gateway controls blocking caller ID modification requests from client applications; (5) server-side call authentication logging with immutable timestamps; (6) user-facing warnings when caller ID cannot be verified; (7) capability to report and block anonymous calls at network termination; (8) regular penetration testing of call routing mechanisms

threat_vector: Cellular module permission management vulnerability leading to unauthorized data access
exploitation_method: Attacker exploits insufficient permission checks in cellular module APIs to access protected user data (SMS, call logs, location, IMEI) without proper authorization, bypassing OS-level permission frameworks through direct module interaction
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unauthorized cellular module API calls without corresponding permission grants; unexpected access to /dev/modem or cellular hardware interfaces; anomalous permission escalation attempts; system calls to cellular functions from unprivileged processes; unexpected data exfiltration patterns via cellular interface; permission denial logs followed by successful data access through alternate paths
mitigation_strategy: Implement mandatory access control (MAC) policies for cellular module operations; enforce explicit permission verification before each sensitive cellular API call; isolate cellular module in separate security domain with strict IPC controls; regular security audits of permission validation logic; apply firmware patches; implement hardware-level access controls; monitor and log all cellular module permission requests; use SELinux or AppArmor policies to restrict cellular access to authorized processes only

threat_vector: WhatsApp calling logic vulnerability (CVE-2021-24112) in Android and iOS versions prior to v2.21.23/v2.21.230
exploitation_method: Attacker sends specially crafted call signal packets that trigger memory corruption or arbitrary code execution through improper validation in the calling subsystem's packet processing logic
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unexpected process crashes in WhatsApp with segmentation faults, unusual memory access patterns in calling module, failed call initialization followed by app restart, network traffic with malformed call control packets (SDP/RTP protocol violations), multiple rapid call attempts from same source to same target within seconds
mitigation_strategy: 1) Enforce automatic app updates to v2.21.23+ for Android or v2.21.230+ for iOS through MDM policies. 2) Block or restrict calling features at network level if updates cannot be deployed immediately. 3) Monitor process execution for WhatsApp crashes and restart behavior. 4) Implement network segmentation to limit exposure from compromised devices. 5) Maintain updated app inventory to identify vulnerable installations.

threat_vector: Improper exception handling in NPU driver (pre-Jan 2022 SMR Release 1) enabling arbitrary memory write and code execution
exploitation_method: Attacker sends specially crafted input to NPU driver that triggers unhandled or improperly handled exceptional conditions, bypassing bounds checking or validation. This allows writing to arbitrary memory locations and potentially executing arbitrary code in kernel context with NPU driver privileges.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) NPU driver crashes or unexpected kernel exceptions followed by memory corruption indicators; (2) Unusual NPU ioctl calls with malformed parameters; (3) Memory access violations in NPU driver address space; (4) Unexpected kernel memory writes originating from NPU driver module; (5) Suspicious DMA transactions or memory mappings initiated by NPU hardware; (6) Kernel log entries showing 'invalid input', 'bounds check failure', or 'NULL pointer dereference' in NPU driver stack traces
mitigation_strategy: Immediately update NPU driver to Jan-2022 SMR Release 1 or later. Implement input validation and bounds checking for all NPU driver ioctl handlers. Use memory tagging or SMMU/IOMMU to isolate NPU DMA access. Deploy kernel address space layout randomization (KASLR) and control flow integrity (CFI) mechanisms. Apply principle of least privilege to NPU driver process capabilities. Restrict user access to NPU device nodes. Enable kernel page table isolation (KPTI) and memory protection extensions (MPE).

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Malware (T1566.001 - Phishing: Spearphishing Attachment)
exploitation_method: Attacker sends crafted email with malicious attachment (executable, macro-enabled document, or archive) that executes code upon user interaction, establishing persistence or lateral movement capability
detection_pattern: Monitor for: suspicious file extensions (.exe, .scr, .vbs in email), Office documents with enabled macros, unusual process execution from %TEMP% or Downloads, parent process anomalies (winword.exe spawning cmd.exe), elevated network connections from unexpected processes, registry modifications for persistence mechanisms
mitigation_strategy: Implement endpoint protection with behavioral analysis, disable macros by default in Office with ADMX policies, enforce email gateway scanning with sandboxing, conduct user security awareness training on phishing, maintain application whitelisting, deploy YARA rules for known malware signatures, enable attack surface reduction rules, require code signing verification, and maintain updated malware definitions

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Proc Filesystem (T1120)
exploitation_method: Attacker reads /proc filesystem entries to enumerate running processes, network connections, environment variables, and system capabilities without requiring elevated privileges. Common targets include /proc/[pid]/cmdline, /proc/[pid]/environ, /proc/net/tcp, and /proc/sys entries to gather intelligence for privilege escalation or lateral movement.
detection_pattern: Monitor for unusual /proc filesystem access patterns: repeated reads of /proc/net/* by non-system users, /proc/[pid]/environ access by unprivileged processes, scanning multiple /proc/[pid]/ directories in short timeframe, access to /proc/sys/kernel/* by unexpected processes. Log via auditd rules on /proc access, correlate with process execution events showing reconnaissance tools (cat, strings, hexdump) targeting proc files.
