detection_pattern: Monitor for: containerd process spawning with unusual SELinux label parameters, audit logs showing SELinux denials being bypassed, containers mounting host paths with unconfined SELinux contexts (type=unconfined_t or permissive domains), unexpected modification of /proc/sys/kernel/ns_last_pid or cgroup escape indicators, container processes accessing host /etc/shadow or /root directories without denial
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade containerd to v1.4.8, v1.5.5, or later versions that properly enforce SELinux policies. Enable audit logging for SELinux violations. Restrict container runtime permissions with seccomp and AppArmor profiles. Implement admission controllers to prevent containers from specifying custom SELinux labels. Run containerd with minimal required capabilities and disable privileged container mode

threat_vector: MediaWiki Information Disclosure - Unprivileged Access to Confidential User Metadata
exploitation_method: Unprivileged users exploit improper access controls in MediaWiki versions before 1.35.5, 1.36.3, and 1.37.1 to access restricted API endpoints or log files that expose IP addresses, User-Agent headers, and other sensitive user metadata through predictable query parameters or direct endpoint access
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unusual API calls to endpoints like api.php with action=query parameters accessing user/revision data from non-administrative accounts; (2) Access logs showing repeated requests to sensitive log files or endpoints by low-privilege users; (3) Anomalous patterns in access to Special:Contributions or revision history with metadata extraction attempts; (4) Increased failed authentication attempts followed by successful metadata queries; (5) Log entries showing User-Agent or IP information being returned to users lacking appropriate permissions
mitigation_strategy: Immediately upgrade to MediaWiki 1.35.5, 1.36.3, 1.37.1 or later; implement strict role-based access controls (RBAC) restricting metadata access to administrators only; configure API permissions to deny sensitive field access for unprivileged users; implement audit logging for metadata access requests; use Web Application Firewall (WAF) rules to block suspicious API queries targeting user metadata; regularly review user permissions and audit logs for unauthorized access patterns

threat_vector: SdLegacySmm SMI handler vulnerability in Insyde InsydeH2O UEFI firmware allowing untrusted code execution via System Management Mode
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious SMI (System Management Interrupt) calls with crafted parameters to bypass input validation in the vulnerable SdLegacySmm handler, achieving arbitrary code execution in SMM context with elevated privileges, potentially compromising firmware integrity and system security
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unusual SMI call sequences in firmware logs, unexpected BIOS/UEFI debug output showing SdLegacySmm handler invocations with suspicious parameters, firmware integrity check failures, unexplained SMM code modifications, host-based detection of SMM entry anomalies via specialized tools, UEFI variable tampering, or changes to System Management Area (SMA) memory regions
mitigation_strategy: Immediately apply firmware updates to patched versions (5.15.11+, 5.25.11+, 5.34.11+, 5.42.11+ respectively), enable SMM protection features if available (SMRR - SMM Range Register), implement BIOS/UEFI code signing verification, restrict SMI handler execution scope, apply input validation patches, enable Secure Boot, monitor firmware with TPM attestation, and implement firmware write protection via hardware mechanisms

threat_vector: GraphQL introspection abuse
exploitation_method: Attacker sends introspection queries to GraphQL endpoints to enumerate schema details, discover hidden fields, mutations, subscriptions, and their argument types. This information is used to craft targeted attacks, identify deprecated or admin-only operations, or find information disclosure vulnerabilities.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: repeated __schema or __type queries in short timeframes, introspection queries from unusual source IPs, introspection requests with high query complexity scores, queries containing introspection directives in high volume, unusual user-agent patterns or automation signatures, access to introspection endpoints from non-authenticated sessions
mitigation_strategy: Disable introspection in production environments; implement authentication/authorization checks before allowing introspection queries; rate-limit introspection requests per IP/user; use query complexity analysis to block resource-intensive introspection attempts; implement Web Application Firewall rules to detect introspection patterns; monitor and log all introspection attempts; use GraphQL middleware to enforce introspection policies; provide sanitized schema documentation through separate, controlled channels instead of live introspection

threat_vector: Stored XSS via wcj_delete_role parameter in Booster for WooCommerce plugin < 5.4.9
exploitation_method: Attacker with admin access crafts malicious request with unsanitized wcj_delete_role parameter containing JavaScript payload. When General module is enabled, parameter is output without sanitization/escaping on admin dashboard, executing arbitrary script in admin context.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) HTTP POST requests containing wcj_delete_role parameter with script tags or event handlers, (2) Dashboard logs showing wcj_delete_role values containing <script>, javascript:, onerror=, onclick= patterns, (3) WordPress admin audit logs of unusual role deletion attempts, (4) WAF alerts for XSS payloads in query parameters, (5) Admin session anomalies or unauthorized admin actions post-injection
mitigation_strategy: Immediately update Booster for WooCommerce to version 5.4.9 or later. Disable General module if update unavailable. Implement input validation: sanitize wcj_delete_role with sanitize_text_field() and validate against known role names. Escape output using esc_html() or esc_attr() before dashboard display. Apply WordPress security hardening: restrict admin dashboard access by IP, enforce strong admin credentials, implement Web Application Firewall rules blocking XSS patterns in admin parameters, enable WordPress security auditing plugins, and conduct code review of plugin parameter handling.

threat_vector: Denial of Service in Binaryen 104 - Assertion abort in wasm::WasmBinaryBuilder::visitRethrow()
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts a malformed WebAssembly binary containing an invalid rethrow instruction that triggers an assertion failure in the visitRethrow() function, causing the Binaryen parser/builder to abort and crash the application processing the WASM module.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Core dumps or application crashes with stack trace containing 'visitRethrow' and assertion failures in Binaryen library code; (2) Sudden termination of WASM compilation/parsing processes; (3) Log entries showing 'Assertion failed' or 'abort()' calls from Binaryen threads; (4) Unexpected exit codes (typically 134 or 139) from processes using Binaryen; (5) Multiple rapid WASM module submissions followed by service unavailability.
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade Binaryen to version 105 or later containing the patch for visitRethrow() validation. Implement input validation: reject WASM binaries with invalid rethrow instructions before passing to Binaryen. Deploy rate limiting on WASM module processing endpoints. Run Binaryen in sandboxed/containerized environments with resource limits and automatic restart policies. Implement watchdog timers to detect and restart hanging WASM compilation processes. Add exception handling wrappers around WASM parsing code to catch and log assertion failures gracefully.

threat_vector: nltk is vulnerable to Inefficient Regular Expression Complexity
exploitation_method: Attacker submits specially crafted input strings to nltk regex operations (tokenization, stemming, or POS tagging) designed to cause catastrophic backtracking in inefficient regex patterns, causing exponential processing time and CPU exhaustion (ReDoS attack)
detection_pattern: Monitor for: sustained high CPU usage by Python nltk processes, increased processing latency for text operations, timeout errors in regex operations, application response times exceeding thresholds by 10x+ for text processing tasks, error logs containing 'regex timeout' or 'catastrophic backtracking' messages, process hang/zombie processes in strace showing regex syscalls in infinite loops
mitigation_strategy: Update nltk to latest patched version; implement input validation with length limits and character whitelisting; add regex timeout mechanisms using signal handlers or timeout libraries; monitor and profile regex operations; use simpler, deterministic regex patterns; implement rate limiting on text processing endpoints; isolate nltk processing in sandboxed containers with CPU/memory limits; consider alternative tokenization libraries with proven ReDoS protections

threat_vector: Race condition in HwConnectivityExService API concurrent call handling leading to denial of service
exploitation_method: Attacker sends multiple concurrent API requests to HwConnectivityExService without proper synchronization, causing race conditions in shared resource access. Unprotected state mutations and missing mutual exclusion lead to memory corruption, null pointer dereferences, or deadlocks that crash the system
detection_pattern: Monitor for: repeated rapid API calls to HwConnectivityExService within millisecond timeframes; system crashes or ANR (Application Not Responding) errors correlated with concurrent requests; memory access violations in HwConnectivityExService logs; thread contention warnings in system logs; state inconsistency errors in connectivity service debug output
mitigation_strategy: Implement thread-safe mechanisms: add mutex/semaphore locks around critical sections in HwConnectivityExService; use atomic operations for shared state; implement connection pooling with bounded concurrency limits; add input validation and rate limiting for API calls; sanitize concurrent request handling with queue-based serialization; apply latest firmware patches; conduct code review focusing on race condition elimination; implement watchdog timers to detect hangs

threat_vector: SVG file upload with malicious content disguised as HTML file in Convos chat application
exploitation_method: Attacker uploads an SVG file with .html extension containing embedded JavaScript or XSS payloads. When another user downloads or views the file through the web browser, the SVG renders with script execution capabilities, potentially stealing session tokens, credentials, or performing actions on behalf of the user.
detection_pattern: Monitor file upload events for: (1) .html files containing SVG MIME type signatures (<svg> tags), (2) SVG content with script elements or event handlers (onload, onclick), (3) Unusual file type mismatches in upload logs, (4) Browser console errors indicating script execution from uploaded files, (5) Requests to external domains originating from file preview/download actions
mitigation_strategy: Implement strict file type validation: (1) Validate file content MIME types server-side, not just extensions, (2) Reject or sanitize SVG files and HTML files containing script tags using a whitelist parser, (3) Serve uploaded files with Content-Type: text/plain and Content-Disposition: attachment to prevent browser rendering, (4) Implement Content Security Policy headers to block inline scripts, (5) Use sandboxed iframe contexts for file previews, (6) Educate users to avoid downloading/opening files from untrusted sources

threat_vector: Taint Shared Content (T1080)
exploitation_method: Attacker modifies files, documents, or media in shared repositories, cloud storage, or collaborative platforms to inject malicious content that will be distributed to multiple users when they access or download the compromised files
detection_pattern: File integrity monitoring alerts on unexpected modifications to shared directories; unusual file size changes; hash mismatches in version control systems; access logs showing modifications from unauthorized accounts; timestamps indicating changes outside business hours; antivirus detections on shared content; user reports of unexpected file versions or corrupted documents
mitigation_strategy: Implement file integrity monitoring and hash verification for shared content; enforce read-only permissions for sensitive shared files where applicable; enable version control and maintain audit trails with immutable logs; use Digital Rights Management and code signing for critical documents; implement email and content scanning before sharing; restrict write permissions to shared repositories by role; maintain frequent backups separate from production systems; educate users to verify file integrity before opening; deploy endpoint detection and response (EDR) solutions to detect suspicious file modifications

threat_vector: Arbitrary File Exposure - Sensitive Information Disclosure
exploitation_method: Attacker gains unauthorized access to files through misconfigured file permissions, directory traversal, insecure direct object references (IDOR), or unprotected file storage endpoints. Sensitive data such as credentials, PII, API keys, or configuration files become accessible to unauthorized actors.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unauthorized file access attempts in access logs, unexpected HTTP requests to sensitive file paths (e.g., /config, /backup, /.env), 403 errors followed by successful 200 responses, unusual file read patterns, access to files outside expected directories, requests with path traversal sequences (../ or encoded variants), failed authentication followed by direct file requests, or access to files with known sensitive naming patterns
mitigation_strategy: Implement principle of least privilege for file permissions; enforce role-based access controls (RBAC); use secure file storage outside web root; implement authentication and authorization checks before file serving; configure web server to deny directory listing; sanitize and validate file path inputs; use access control lists (ACLs); encrypt sensitive files at rest; implement audit logging for file access; conduct regular file permission audits; use Content Security Policy headers; implement rate limiting on file requests

threat_vector: ReDoS via malicious regex input
exploitation_method: Attacker submits specially crafted input strings designed to trigger catastrophic backtracking in vulnerable regex patterns. Common patterns include nested quantifiers (a+a+b) or alternation with overlapping matches (a|ab). When regex engine processes these strings, it exhausts CPU resources attempting exponential combinations of matches, causing denial of service.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Sudden CPU spikes correlated with specific input parameters, (2) Regex operations exceeding normal execution time thresholds (>1s), (3) Request patterns with repetitive character sequences, (4) Increased thread/process count during input validation phases, (5) Application logs showing timeout errors in regex operations, (6) Web application firewall logs flagging suspicious regex-like payloads with nested quantifiers
mitigation_strategy: Implement: (1) Regex timeout limits at application level (e.g., 100-500ms max execution time), (2) Use regex validation libraries designed for ReDoS protection (safe-regex, rxxr2), (3) Replace complex regex with simpler patterns or state machines, (4) Apply input length limits and character whitelisting pre-validation, (5) Deploy WAF rules blocking common ReDoS patterns, (6) Use non-backtracking regex engines (e.g., .NET RegexOptions.NonBacktracking), (7) Rate limit requests with suspicious regex patterns, (8) Monitor and alert on regex execution time anomalies

threat_vector: eID module null pointer dereference vulnerability leading to information disclosure
exploitation_method: Attacker supplies malformed eID input or triggers specific code path that causes the module to dereference an uninitialized or incorrectly validated pointer, reading adjacent memory regions containing sensitive authentication data or cryptographic material
detection_pattern: Monitor for: segmentation faults in eID processes, abnormal memory access patterns, repeated crashes of eID service with specific input patterns, unexpected data leakage in error logs, memory dumps containing credential material, and failed pointer validation checks in security logs
mitigation_strategy: Implement null pointer checks before all pointer dereferences in eID module; enable compiler-level protections (AddressSanitizer, -fno-delete-null-pointer-checks); conduct static analysis and fuzzing of eID input parsing; apply bounds checking and input validation; isolate eID module in sandboxed process with restricted memory access; maintain comprehensive error handling without exposing memory contents; keep eID library versions updated with security patches

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Credentials in Registry (T1552.002)
exploitation_method: Attacker searches Windows Registry for stored credentials, connection strings, or authentication tokens in keys like HKLM/HKCU/Software. Common targets include RDP credentials, SQL connection strings, VPN configs, and application API keys stored unencrypted or with weak obfuscation in registry hives.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: reg query commands targeting HKCU/HKLM Software paths; process creation with reg.exe or PowerShell accessing credential-related registry keys; unusual registry read patterns from non-admin processes; Event ID 4657 (Registry value modified) for sensitive keys; Sysmon Event ID 12-13 for registry access to known credential storage locations (e.g., HKCU\Software\SimonTatham, \Putty, \OpenSSH, VPN software keys)
mitigation_strategy: 1) Restrict registry access via GPO for sensitive hives; 2) Enforce credential storage in Windows Credential Manager or dedicated vaults instead of registry; 3) Enable LSA Protection and Credential Guard; 4) Use DPAPI with strong encryption for any stored credentials; 5) Implement registry access auditing and alerting on sensitive keys; 6) Conduct regular registry audits to identify unencrypted credentials; 7) Apply principle of least privilege to limit process-level registry access; 8) Use password managers for application credentials instead of hardcoding in registry

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Group Policy Discovery
exploitation_method: Attacker uses commands like `gpresult`, `Get-GPO`, `Get-GroupPolicy` PowerShell cmdlets, or LDAP queries to enumerate Group Policy Objects (GPOs) and their applied settings across Active Directory to identify security gaps, delegation models, and configuration weaknesses for lateral movement or privilege escalation
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unusual PowerShell cmdlet execution from non-admin accounts (Get-GPO, Get-GPOReport, Get-ChildItem on SYSVOL); (2) LDAP query spikes targeting cn=policies in Active Directory; (3) gpresult.exe execution with /h or /scope parameters; (4) Access to \\<domain>\SYSVOL\<domain>\Policies directories from unexpected source IPs; (5) Event ID 5136 (AD object modified) on GPO containers from non-DC sources; (6) Process creation events for dsquery.exe or ldifde.exe targeting policy objects
mitigation_strategy: Implement: (1) Restrict PowerShell script block logging and constrained language mode for non-privileged users; (2) Limit SYSVOL share access via SMB signing enforcement and access controls; (3) Monitor and alert on Get-GPO/Get-ChildItem cmdlet execution; (4) Use AppLocker to prevent unauthorized gpresult.exe execution; (5) Deploy Group Policy audit logging (Audit Policy Change events); (6) Implement network segmentation limiting LDAP query sources; (7) Use security filtering on GPOs to restrict enumeration permissions; (8) Enable Windows Defender for Endpoint detection of living-off-the-land enumeration patterns

threat_vector: CaasKit module path traversal vulnerability leading to application unavailability in MeeTime
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious file path requests using directory traversal sequences (../, ..\ or encoded variants) to access files outside the intended directory. By targeting critical configuration, runtime, or system files, the attacker can corrupt or delete essential application resources, causing denial of service.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) HTTP requests containing path traversal patterns (%2e%2e, ../, encoded backslashes) in URL parameters or file paths; (2) Unusual file system access attempts by the MeeTime/CaasKit process targeting directories outside application root; (3) Failed file access attempts followed by application errors or crashes; (4) Log entries showing access to sensitive system paths (etc/, windows/system32, etc.) through CaasKit endpoints; (5) Sudden increase in 40x HTTP status codes or application exceptions related to file operations.
mitigation_strategy: Implement strict input validation on all file path parameters: (1) Use allowlist-based path validation against a whitelist of permitted directories; (2) Canonicalize all file paths and verify they remain within the designated base directory; (3) Disable or restrict directory traversal characters in input; (4) Run MeeTime/CaasKit with minimal file system permissions using least-privilege principles; (5) Apply principle of least privilege to service accounts; (6) Deploy a Web Application Firewall (WAF) with rules to block path traversal patterns; (7) Update CaasKit module to patched version; (8) Implement file system access controls and monitoring; (9) Maintain file integrity monitoring on critical application files.

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1114 - Email Collection
exploitation_method: Attacker gains access to email systems via compromised credentials, insider threat, or vulnerable email client/server. Attacker then exports mailbox contents, uses email forwarding rules, or accesses shared mailboxes to exfiltrate messages, attachments, and metadata for espionage or credential harvesting.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unusual mailbox access outside business hours, bulk email exports, creation of forwarding rules by non-standard accounts, IMAP/POP3 connections from anomalous IPs, excessive attachment downloads, abnormal PowerShell mailbox cmdlet usage (New-InboxRule, Export-Mailbox), changes to delegate permissions, accessing shared mailboxes by unusual accounts, spike in email read operations within short timeframe
mitigation_strategy: Implement conditional access policies restricting mailbox access by location/device, enforce MFA on email accounts, disable legacy authentication protocols (IMAP/POP3), monitor and alert on mailbox forwarding/delegate changes, use email DLP to block bulk exports, conduct regular mailbox audits and permission reviews, segment email infrastructure, maintain security awareness training, implement mailbox search auditing, and enforce principle of least privilege for shared mailbox access

threat_vector: Null pointer dereference in thread profile trap handler due to missing thread ID validation before dereferencing in Snapdragon platforms
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malformed interrupt or trap that passes invalid/null thread ID to profile trap handler, causing kernel to attempt dereferencing null pointer, resulting in denial of service or potential code execution if memory layout is predictable
detection_pattern: Monitor for kernel panic/oops messages referencing thread profile handler; detect unexpected null pointer dereference exceptions in kernel logs; identify trap handler invocations with thread ID values of 0 or invalid ranges; observe sudden process terminations with segmentation fault signals; track syscall patterns attempting to trigger profile traps with invalid context
mitigation_strategy: Implement mandatory thread ID validation before any dereference operation in trap handler (bounds checking, null checks, thread structure validity verification); use static analysis tools to identify all dereference paths; add runtime assertions for thread pointer validity; implement proper error handling with graceful failure instead of dereferencing; apply vendor security patches; enable kernel address space layout randomization (KASLR) and stack canaries to reduce exploitation window

threat_vector: Stored XSS in Vehicle Service Management System 1.0 - Service Requests Section
exploitation_method: Attacker injects malicious JavaScript payload into Service Requests form fields (e.g., service description, notes, or customer name) that are stored in the database without proper sanitization or encoding. When other users view the stored request, the payload executes in their browser context, allowing session hijacking, credential theft, or administrative actions.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) POST/GET requests to service request endpoints containing script tags, event handlers (onclick, onerror, onload), or JavaScript protocol URLs; (2) Database logs showing unusual character sequences like '<script>', 'javascript:', or encoded variants (%3Cscript%3E); (3) Anomalous DOM modifications or unexpected network requests from service request display pages; (4) WAF alerts for HTML/JavaScript injection attempts; (5) HTTP response headers missing Content-Security-Policy or X-XSS-Protection directives.
mitigation_strategy: Implement: (1) Input validation - whitelist allowed characters for each field, reject or sanitize script tags and event handlers; (2) Output encoding - HTML-encode all user-supplied data before rendering (use templating engines with auto-escape); (3) Content Security Policy headers to prevent inline script execution; (4) HTTPOnly and Secure flags on session cookies; (5) Regular security audits and penetration testing; (6) Update to patched version when available; (7) Apply principle of least privilege for database access; (8) Implement a Web Application Firewall with XSS detection rules.

threat_vector: Apache Avro .NET SDK Resource Exhaustion Vulnerability (CVE-2023-39615)
exploitation_method: Attacker sends maliciously crafted Avro-serialized messages with nested structures or large array declarations that cause unbounded memory allocation during deserialization, exhausting heap memory and CPU resources
detection_pattern: Monitor for: sudden spikes in memory consumption during Avro deserialization operations, repeated out-of-memory exceptions in application logs, CPU utilization approaching 100%, abnormal growth of GC (garbage collection) heap size, network traffic containing Avro messages with unusually deep nesting levels or excessively large block counts
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade Apache Avro .NET SDK to patched version (1.11.1 or later); implement input validation to enforce maximum message size limits and nesting depth restrictions; configure memory quotas and resource limits at the application level; use connection timeouts and rate limiting to prevent sustained resource exhaustion; enable detailed logging and alerting on deserialization failures; isolate Avro processing in containerized environments with defined resource constraints

threat_vector: Integer overflow in build_model function of xmlparse.c in Expat library versions before 2.4.3
exploitation_method: Attacker provides maliciously crafted XML with deeply nested elements or excessive attribute counts that causes integer overflow in memory allocation calculations within the build_model function, leading to heap buffer overflow or memory corruption
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Expat library version detection showing < 2.4.3, (2) XML parsing processes with abnormally large nesting depths (>10000 levels), (3) Heap corruption signals in application logs, (4) Segmentation faults or access violations during XML parsing, (5) Memory allocation failures followed by continued parsing attempts, (6) Core dumps with stack traces pointing to expat xmlparse.c build_model function
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade Expat library to version 2.4.3 or later immediately. Implement XML input validation with strict limits: maximum nesting depth (recommend ≤1024), maximum document size, and maximum attribute count per element. Deploy resource limits on XML parsing processes. Use security scanning in build pipeline to detect Expat versions < 2.4.3. Apply WAF rules to reject malformed XML. Monitor application behavior for parser crashes and implement graceful error handling for parsing exceptions.

threat_vector: Duplicate CVE identifier exploitation
exploitation_method: Attackers reference outdated or duplicate CVE identifiers to confuse vulnerability tracking systems, causing security teams to miss critical patches by directing them to incorrect or deprecated CVE numbers in vulnerability databases
detection_pattern: Monitor for CVE references that resolve to 'duplicate' or 'do not use' statuses in NVD/MITRE databases; alert on patch management systems receiving conflicting CVE identifiers for the same vulnerability; track tickets referencing deprecated CVE IDs in security workflows
mitigation_strategy: Implement CVE validation logic that cross-references against official MITRE/NVD databases before processing; maintain canonical vulnerability mappings that redirect duplicate CVEs to primary identifiers; require security alerts to verify CVE status before dispatch; use automated tools to normalize CVE references to their primary identifiers

threat_vector: Prototype pollution in JavaScript
exploitation_method: Attacker injects malicious properties into Object.prototype or other built-in prototypes by manipulating user-controlled input that gets recursively merged into objects, typically through merge operations on JSON payloads or query parameters. This causes all object instances to inherit poisoned properties, potentially overwriting critical application logic or security controls.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) POST/PUT requests with nested '__proto__', 'constructor', or 'prototype' keys in JSON payloads; (2) Unusual property assignments to Object.prototype detected via code analysis; (3) Unexpected property presence on plain objects in runtime monitoring; (4) Access logs showing requests with encoded versions like '__proto__' or 'constructor.prototype'; (5) Application behavior changes like disabled input validation or authentication bypass without code changes
mitigation_strategy: Implement input sanitization by: (1) Rejecting or removing '__proto__', 'constructor', 'prototype', and 'length' keys from untrusted input before deserialization; (2) Using Object.create(null) for objects that should not inherit from Object.prototype; (3) Employing safe merge libraries with built-in prototype pollution protection; (4) Validating object property assignments against whitelists; (5) Using Object.defineProperty with non-configurable flags for critical properties; (6) Implementing Content Security Policy and disabling dynamic property access in security-sensitive contexts

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1592: Gather Victim Host Information
exploitation_method: Attacker performs passive or active reconnaissance on target systems to identify OS versions, installed software, hardware specifications, and network configuration details through public sources (job listings, social media), network scanning tools (nmap, Shodan queries), or direct probing of services to fingerprint systems before launching targeted attacks
detection_pattern: Unusual outbound DNS queries to reconnaissance services; repeated failed connection attempts to various ports on internal systems; suspicious user-agent strings in web logs; network traffic to Shodan/Censys-like APIs from internal hosts; abnormal ICMP or UDP probe patterns; excessive service enumeration queries within short timeframes; scanning tools' distinctive packet signatures (SYN scans, version probes); logs showing multiple requests to banners or version endpoints across different services
mitigation_strategy: Implement network segmentation and firewall rules blocking reconnaissance tool communications; disable unnecessary service banners and version information disclosure; use intrusion detection systems (IDS) configured to alert on scanning patterns; monitor outbound connections to known reconnaissance platforms; implement endpoint detection and response (EDR) to flag scanning tools; conduct regular security awareness training to prevent information leakage through public profiles; maintain an asset inventory to identify what information is publicly exposed; use DNS sinkholing for reconnaissance infrastructure domains

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Digital Certificates (T1187)
exploitation_method: Attacker obtains or forges SSL/TLS certificates through compromised CAs, self-signed certificates, or valid certificates for domains they control to establish encrypted communications that appear legitimate, enabling MITM attacks, C2 channels, or phishing infrastructure.
detection_pattern: Certificate Transparency (CT) log anomalies for your domains, self-signed certificates in trust stores, expired or revoked certificates still in use, mismatched CN/SAN values, unusual certificate issuance from unexpected CAs, browser certificate warnings, unexpected certificates in network traffic captured by proxies/firewalls.
mitigation_strategy: Deploy certificate pinning for critical applications, monitor CT logs for unauthorized issuance, implement strict CA policies and DNSSEC, use OCSP stapling and CRL checking, enforce certificate validation in all TLS connections, restrict trusted root CAs, deploy network-based certificate inspection, maintain certificate inventory and audit trails, and implement multi-factor authentication for CA account access.

threat_vector: Incorrect Knox Guard implementation pre-SMR Jan-2022 allows physical attackers to unlock device via Samsung DeX mode
exploitation_method: Attacker with physical access connects device to DeX dock/USB-C display. Due to improper Knox Guard state validation during DeX mode transition, Knox security context is temporarily bypassed, allowing unauthorized unlock without credentials
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unexpected Knox Guard state transitions to 'disabled' or 'reduced' during DeX mode activation; SELinux policy violations in Knox logs; unauthorized unlock events without biometric/PIN authentication; unexpected dex_service process permission escalations; Knox integrity measurement architecture (IMA) anomalies; rapid state changes between secure and non-secure boot states in Knox audit logs
mitigation_strategy: 1) Immediately patch to SMR Jan-2022 Release 1 or later; 2) Restrict physical access to devices and monitor for unauthorized DeX connections; 3) Enable Knox real-time protection and ensure Knox Guard runtime integrity verification is active; 4) Implement device management policies to disable DeX mode if not required; 5) Monitor Knox audit logs for anomalous mode transitions; 6) Use Knox Vault for sensitive data to provide additional isolation layer independent of unlock state

threat_vector: ATT&CK: SAML Tokens (T1187)
exploitation_method: Attacker forges or steals SAML tokens to impersonate legitimate users and gain unauthorized access to federated applications. Methods include token theft via network interception, XML signature wrapping attacks, or compromising identity provider (IdP) signing keys.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) SAML assertions with invalid or missing digital signatures, (2) tokens issued by untrusted IdPs, (3) assertions with modified NameID or claims, (4) token usage from anomalous IPs or timezones, (5) repeated failed token validation attempts, (6) XML parsing errors in SAML processing logs, (7) tokens with expiration times far in the future
mitigation_strategy: Implement: (1) strict SAML signature validation with certificate pinning, (2) enforce encrypted assertions and TLS for all token transport, (3) validate token issuer and audience restrictions, (4) implement short token expiration times (5-10 minutes), (5) deploy network segmentation to isolate IdP communication, (6) enable SAML-specific logging and centralized monitoring, (7) use strong cryptographic algorithms (SHA-256+), (8) implement replay attack detection, (9) enforce multi-factor authentication as secondary validation, (10) regularly audit and update IdP signing certificates

threat_vector: Pointer Dereference in GPAC 1.0.1 unlink_chunk.isra function causing Denial of Service
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malformed MP4 or similar media file with corrupted chunk metadata that causes unlink_chunk.isra to dereference a null or invalid pointer, triggering memory access violation and process crash
detection_pattern: Process crash with SIGSEGV/access violation in unlink_chunk.isra; segmentation fault logs referencing chunk unlinking operations; abnormal termination of media processing services; memory sanitizer reports of invalid pointer dereference in libgpac; sudden service unavailability after processing specific media files
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade GPAC to version 1.0.2 or later with pointer validation patches; implement input validation for media file chunk structures before processing; run GPAC in sandboxed environment with memory protection; deploy file format fuzzing tests; monitor process crashes and implement automatic service restart with rate limiting; use AddressSanitizer during development and testing

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1543.003 - Create or Modify System Process: Windows Service
exploitation_method: Attacker uses sc.exe, PowerShell, or direct Registry modification to create malicious Windows services or modify existing service binaries/configurations to execute arbitrary code with SYSTEM privileges at startup
detection_pattern: Monitor for: new service creation events (Event ID 7045), sc.exe/powershell.exe execution with service parameters, Registry modifications to HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Services, unexpected ImagePath changes, services pointing to suspicious directories (%temp%, AppData), services with no description or standard naming conventions
mitigation_strategy: Implement application whitelisting for service binaries, restrict sc.exe and service management tool execution via Group Policy, monitor and alert on Service Control Manager events, use Windows Defender for Endpoint behavioral detection, maintain service inventory with CodeSign enforcement, disable unnecessary services, apply principle of least privilege to service accounts

threat_vector: GDAL 3.3.0-3.4.0 PCIDSK heap-based buffer overflow (CVE-2021-21898)
exploitation_method: Attacker provides malformed PCIDSK image file with crafted segment headers containing oversized data lengths. When GDAL parses the file through CPCIDSKFile::ReadFromFile or CPCIDSKBinarySegment constructor, insufficient bounds checking allows writing beyond allocated heap buffer, enabling code execution or denial of service.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) GDAL process crashes with segmentation faults when processing PCIDSK files; (2) unusual heap allocations followed by write operations exceeding buffer size in PCIDSK parsing code paths; (3) file access attempts to .pix files with abnormally large segment declaration headers; (4) GDAL child process termination with non-zero exit codes during image processing workflows; (5) AddressSanitizer or similar tool reports of heap-buffer-overflow in PCIDSK::CPCIDSKSegment or PCIDSK::CPCIDSKBinarySegment functions.
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade GDAL to version 3.4.1 or later which includes bounds checking fixes. For legacy deployments: (1) disable PCIDSK driver if unused (build GDAL without PCIDSK support); (2) validate and sanitize all PCIDSK input files before processing with dedicated file validators; (3) run GDAL in sandboxed/containerized environment with resource limits; (4) implement file size and segment header validation before passing to GDAL; (5) apply input validation to reject PCIDSK files with segment sizes exceeding reasonable limits; (6) monitor GDAL process resource usage and terminate on anomalies.

threat_vector: Null pointer dereference in smartphone camera module
exploitation_method: Attacker sends specially crafted input (malformed image file, invalid camera parameters, or malicious Intent) to camera application or framework, triggering unhandled null pointer exception that causes service crash or memory corruption
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unexpected camera process crashes with NullPointerException in logs, repeated camera service restarts, ANR (Application Not Responding) events in camera-related processes, SELinux denials involving camera HAL, abnormal memory access patterns in camera framework, crash dumps showing null dereference at known vulnerable code locations
mitigation_strategy: Implement null pointer checks and validation at all camera API entry points; use static code analysis tools to identify potential null dereferences; apply vendor security patches promptly; implement exception handling and graceful degradation for camera failures; enable Address Sanitizer (ASan) in development builds; use defensive programming practices with assertions; implement camera service watchdog with automatic recovery; apply SELinux policies restricting camera module capabilities

threat_vector: ATT&CK: SMS Pumping (T1644)
exploitation_method: Attacker triggers multiple SMS-based verifications or password resets on victim accounts to generate billable SMS traffic. Victims are charged per SMS received while attackers profit from SMS aggregator commissions or cause financial/operational damage through resource exhaustion.
detection_pattern: Spike in SMS delivery requests from single user account; multiple failed or repeated SMS verification attempts within short timeframe; unusual geographic distribution of SMS requests; alerts from SMS gateway provider on abnormal traffic volume; logs showing rapid successive password reset requests; COGS increase in SMS billing with corresponding user activity anomalies
mitigation_strategy: Implement rate limiting on SMS sending (max requests per user per hour); add CAPTCHA to sensitive SMS-triggered actions; require email or push notification verification before SMS reset; monitor SMS costs and set billing alerts; use SMS-less authentication (TOTP, push notifications, hardware keys); implement cooldown periods between verification attempts; verify user consent through secondary channel before sending SMS; maintain blocklist of known SMS pumping patterns and integrate with SMS provider abuse detection systems

threat_vector: HwNearbyMain module NULL Pointer Dereference vulnerability leading to process restart
exploitation_method: Attacker sends specially crafted packets or triggers specific operational sequences that cause the HwNearbyMain module to dereference an uninitialized or freed pointer, resulting in a null reference exception and process crash
detection_pattern: Monitor for: segmentation faults or access violation exceptions in HwNearbyMain process logs; repeated sudden termination and restart cycles of HwNearbyMain service; kernel panic logs referencing HwNearbyMain memory access violations; abnormal nearby device discovery requests followed by process crashes; elevated frequency of service restart events within short time windows
mitigation_strategy: Apply security patches that implement proper null pointer validation and bounds checking in HwNearbyMain module; add defensive pointer sanity checks before dereferencing; implement process monitoring and automatic restart throttling to prevent denial of service; use compiler-based protections like AddressSanitizer during development; maintain updated firmware versions; restrict access to nearby discovery protocols through network segmentation and access controls

threat_vector: Apache James ManagedSieve Path Traversal via Sieve Script Storage
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious sieve script filenames containing path traversal sequences (../) to escape the intended script storage directory, enabling arbitrary file read/write access on the filesystem with James process privileges
detection_pattern: Monitor for: sieve script upload requests with ../ or ..%2F in filenames; file access logs showing James process accessing files outside designated sieve directories; sieve storage directory traversal attempts in application logs; unexpected file modifications in system directories; error messages indicating invalid path resolution during sieve script operations
mitigation_strategy: Update Apache James to patched version; implement strict input validation rejecting path traversal sequences in sieve script filenames; enforce chroot/containerization limiting James filesystem access; use Java SecurityManager restricting file operations to sieve storage directory; regularly audit file permissions on James process; monitor sieve script directory for unauthorized access patterns; implement file integrity monitoring on critical system files

threat_vector: PIL.ImageMath.eval arbitrary code execution via expression evaluation in Pillow < 9.0.0
exploitation_method: Attacker passes malicious Python expressions to PIL.ImageMath.eval() containing exec() calls or lambda functions that execute arbitrary code with application privileges. Example: eval('exec("malicious_code")') or eval('(lambda: __import__("os").system("cmd"))()') when processing untrusted image data or user-supplied parameters.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Pillow version < 9.0.0 in dependency checks, (2) Application logs showing PIL.ImageMath.eval() calls with dynamic/user-controlled arguments, (3) Unusual child process spawning from image processing operations, (4) Network connections initiated during image processing, (5) File system modifications during image operations, (6) Stack traces containing 'PIL.ImageMath' and 'exec' or 'lambda', (7) Regex patterns in logs: ImageMath.eval.*exec|ImageMath.eval.*lambda|__import__.*os
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade Pillow to version 9.0.0 or later (preferred solution). If upgrade impossible: (1) Never pass user-controlled input to PIL.ImageMath.eval(), (2) Implement strict input validation and allowlisting for any expression parameters, (3) Use PIL in a sandboxed environment with restricted permissions, (4) Disable PIL.ImageMath functionality if unused, (5) Apply WAF rules to block requests containing 'exec', 'lambda', '__import__' in image processing parameters, (6) Implement application-level expression evaluation restrictions using ast.literal_eval for safe-only operations

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Application Access Token (T1550.001)
exploitation_method: Attacker obtains valid application access tokens through credential theft, phishing, or compromised development environments, then uses these tokens to authenticate API calls and access protected resources without triggering standard credential-based alerts
detection_pattern: Anomalous API calls from unusual geographic locations or IP addresses; access tokens used outside expected time windows; tokens accessing resources inconsistent with token issuer's typical permissions; sudden spike in API requests using valid credentials; tokens reused across multiple applications or services; access patterns deviating from baseline user behavior; token usage in headless environments or non-interactive sessions
mitigation_strategy: Implement token rotation policies with short expiration windows; enforce conditional access policies based on risk factors; monitor and log all token issuance and usage; require multi-factor authentication for sensitive API operations; implement rate limiting on API endpoints; use token binding to restrict usage to specific clients/IPs; maintain allowlists of approved applications; correlate token usage with user activity logs; implement anomaly detection for token access patterns; revoke compromised tokens immediately; use hardware-based token storage where possible

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1542.001 - Bootkit
exploitation_method: Attacker gains elevated privileges to modify the Master Boot Record (MBR), Volume Boot Record (VBR), or UEFI firmware to inject malicious code that executes before OS kernel initialization, persisting across reboots and evading detection
detection_pattern: Firmware integrity check failures, unexpected MBR/VBR modifications detected by UEFI Secure Boot validation, EventID 6279 (Code Integrity operational log errors), unexpected SMM memory access patterns, discrepancies between firmware manifests and actual firmware contents, BIOS/UEFI update logs showing unauthorized changes, system startup delays or boot sequence anomalies
mitigation_strategy: Enable and properly configure UEFI Secure Boot with verified signing certificates; implement firmware integrity monitoring tools (e.g., chipsec); restrict administrative access to firmware settings with strong authentication; regularly validate firmware checksums against trusted baseline; maintain updated BIOS/UEFI from trusted vendors only; enforce Windows code integrity policies; use Trusted Platform Module (TPM) 2.0 for measured boot; implement endpoint detection and response (EDR) with firmware-level monitoring

threat_vector: Discourse /message-bus/_diagnostics Denial of Service via admin user exploitation
exploitation_method: An authenticated admin user sends repeated or specially crafted requests to the /message-bus/_diagnostics endpoint, which lacks proper rate limiting or resource controls. This causes excessive CPU/memory consumption or message queue flooding, rendering the service unavailable to legitimate users.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unusual spike in requests to /message-bus/_diagnostics endpoint in access logs; (2) High HTTP request volume from admin user accounts; (3) Server resource exhaustion (CPU >80%, memory pressure) correlating with diagnostic endpoint access; (4) Increased processing time for message-bus operations; (5) Queue buildup in diagnostic logs; (6) Admin user making requests outside normal maintenance windows
mitigation_strategy: Apply these countermeasures: (1) Update Discourse to patched version addressing this vulnerability; (2) Implement rate limiting on /message-bus/_diagnostics endpoint regardless of authentication status; (3) Restrict access to diagnostic endpoints via IP whitelist or additional authorization checks beyond admin role; (4) Monitor and alert on resource usage spikes; (5) Implement request timeouts on diagnostic handlers; (6) Audit admin account activities and revoke unnecessary admin privileges; (7) Deploy Web Application Firewall rules to throttle requests to diagnostic paths

threat_vector: Masquerade File Type (T1036.004)
exploitation_method: Attacker modifies file extensions or MIME types to disguise malicious executables as benign documents (e.g., .exe renamed to .pdf, double extensions like .pdf.exe, or altering magic bytes). Users execute malware believing they are opening legitimate files.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: mismatched file extensions vs. actual file signatures (magic bytes), processes spawned from suspicious file locations, double extensions in file names, PE headers in files with document extensions, file type metadata conflicts in endpoint logs, unusual file creation in temp directories with misleading names
mitigation_strategy: Enforce file type validation using actual magic bytes not just extensions; configure Windows to always show file extensions and hide known file types selectively; implement application whitelisting to prevent unsigned executables; block execution from suspicious directories (Downloads, Temp); educate users on verifying file properties; use endpoint detection and response (EDR) with behavioral analysis; configure browser/email filters to block double-extension files

threat_vector: Null pointer dereference in trap handler due to missing thread ID validation in Snapdragon platform kernels
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious input or triggers exception handling path that passes invalid/null thread ID to trap handler. Handler dereferences thread pointer without validation, causing kernel panic, information disclosure, or privilege escalation.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: kernel panic logs with trap handler in stack trace, null pointer dereference exceptions, unexpected thread ID values (0x0 or invalid addresses) in exception logs, abnormal exception handler behavior patterns, memory access violations in kernel trap handling code paths
mitigation_strategy: Implement mandatory thread ID validation before any dereference in trap handlers: verify thread ID is non-null and within valid range, use bounds checking, implement defensive null checks, add assertion statements, enable kernel pointer authentication if available, apply static analysis tools to identify missing validations, implement SMMU/IOMMU protections, keep Snapdragon firmware and kernel patches current

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1218.011 - Signed Binary Proxy Execution: Rundll32
exploitation_method: Attacker executes rundll32.exe with malicious DLL or script parameters to bypass application whitelisting and execute arbitrary code while appearing as a legitimate Windows process
detection_pattern: Process creation events showing rundll32.exe with: non-standard DLL paths (temp folders, AppData, user writable locations), script file extensions (.js, .vbs, .ps1) as parameters, network connections initiated by rundll32, unusual parent processes, command line obfuscation or encoding, DLL loading from SMB shares or remote locations
mitigation_strategy: Implement application whitelisting to restrict rundll32 execution, disable rundll32 entry points via Group Policy (gpedit.msc: Computer Configuration > Administrative Templates > Windows Components > Application Compatibility), monitor and block rundll32 process execution via endpoint detection and response (EDR), enforce code signing validation, restrict DLL search order, disable legacy shell script engines (.vbs, .js), and log all rundll32 command line executions for forensic analysis

threat_vector: WP Travel Engine WordPress plugin < 5.3.1 - Stored XSS via unescaped Description field in Trip Destination/Activities/Trip Type and Pricing Category pages
exploitation_method: Authenticated attacker with low-privilege role (Contributor or Editor) injects malicious JavaScript into the Description field. Due to lack of output escaping, the payload executes in the context of admin pages and affects all site visitors viewing these pages, enabling session hijacking, credential theft, or malware distribution.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) POST requests to wp-admin pages with suspicious script tags in form data (wp_nonce validation present but output not escaped); (2) Database queries showing Description fields containing <script>, javascript:, onerror=, onclick= patterns; (3) Admin audit logs showing low-privilege users modifying Trip Destination/Activities/Pricing fields; (4) Browser console errors or unexpected DOM modifications on frontend pages displaying these fields; (5) WAF alerts on unescaped HTML in wp-admin POST parameters.
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade WP Travel Engine to version 5.3.1 or later. Implement output escaping on all Description fields using wp_kses_post() or esc_html(). Apply capability restrictions limiting Description field editing to 'manage_options' role only. Deploy Content Security Policy headers preventing inline script execution. Implement input validation and sanitization using sanitize_textarea_field(). Conduct admin user audit to identify suspicious modifications in affected fields. Consider Web Application Firewall rules to detect XSS patterns in travel plugin POST requests.

threat_vector: WOOCS WordPress plugin <1.3.7.3 Reflected XSS via custom_prices parameter
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious URL with unsanitized custom_prices parameter containing JavaScript payload. When victim visits the link, the payload executes in their browser context with victim's privileges, potentially stealing session tokens or performing actions on their behalf.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: HTTP requests to WOOCS plugin endpoints with custom_prices parameter containing script tags, event handlers (onerror=, onclick=), or encoded JavaScript; Review access logs for URLs with suspicious parameter values; Check browser console logs for JavaScript execution errors; Monitor cookie exfiltration attempts in outbound requests; Look for unusual admin actions performed by compromised sessions
mitigation_strategy: 1) Immediately update WOOCS to version 1.3.7.3 or later; 2) Implement input validation on custom_prices parameter - whitelist only numeric values and expected formats; 3) Apply output encoding - HTML entity encode all user-controlled data before rendering in responses; 4) Deploy WAF rules to block requests with script tags or event handlers in custom_prices; 5) Implement Content Security Policy (CSP) headers to prevent inline script execution; 6) Use httpOnly and Secure flags on session cookies; 7) Conduct security audit of all plugin parameters for similar XSS vulnerabilities

threat_vector: Hardcoded cryptographic key in ControlUp Real-Time Agent (cuAgent.exe) versions before 8.2.5 enabling unauthorized WCF command execution
exploitation_method: Attacker discovers hardcoded key in binary, uses it to authenticate to WCF (Windows Communication Foundation) service, sends malicious serialized objects or command payloads through authenticated channel to execute arbitrary OS commands with agent privileges
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) cuAgent.exe listening on unexpected WCF endpoints; (2) WCF channel authentication using known hardcoded credentials; (3) Unusual cuAgent.exe child processes (cmd.exe, powershell.exe); (4) cuAgent.exe network connections to unfamiliar internal/external hosts; (5) Event log entries showing cuAgent.exe spawning system commands; (6) Abnormal WCF deserialization of untrusted types in application logs
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade ControlUp Real-Time Agent to version 8.2.5 or later (which removes hardcoded keys and implements proper key management); implement WCF endpoint security policies (message-level encryption, custom authentication); restrict WCF service accessibility via firewall/network segmentation; deploy application whitelisting to prevent unauthorized cuAgent child processes; enable and monitor Windows Event Tracing for WCF activity; apply principle of least privilege to agent service account

threat_vector: Segmentation Fault in GPAC 1.0.1 co64_box_new function causing Denial of Service
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts a malicious MP4 file with a specially constructed co64 (64-bit chunk offset) box that triggers improper memory allocation or bounds checking in the co64_box_new function, causing the GPAC media player to dereference invalid memory and crash
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) GPAC process crashes with SIGSEGV signal; (2) core dumps referencing co64_box_new in stack trace; (3) sudden termination of gpac/mp4box processes when processing specific media files; (4) abnormal memory access patterns in address sanitizer logs; (5) file upload attempts with oversized or malformed co64 box structures
mitigation_strategy: Update GPAC to version >1.0.1 that includes bounds checking fixes; implement input validation for MP4 box headers before processing; run GPAC in sandboxed environments with memory protection; disable automatic media file processing from untrusted sources; deploy file type validation at ingestion points; configure system to limit core dumps and prevent information leakage

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1007 - System Service Discovery
exploitation_method: Attacker enumerates running services, processes, and open ports using native OS commands (tasklist, Get-Service, netstat, ss, systemctl) or reconnaissance tools to identify running applications and potential attack targets for lateral movement or privilege escalation
detection_pattern: Monitor for suspicious execution of discovery commands: tasklist.exe, Get-Service, Get-Process, netstat with unusual flags, ss commands, systemctl with list options, lsof; watch for rapid sequential process queries; examine command line arguments for wildcard patterns or enumeration attempts; alert on discovery tool execution from unusual parent processes or user contexts
mitigation_strategy: Implement application whitelisting to restrict execution of discovery tools; disable or restrict PowerShell execution policies and constrain Get-Service/Get-Process cmdlets via role-based access controls; monitor and alert on netstat/ss execution; use EDR solutions to detect process enumeration patterns; segment network to limit lateral movement post-discovery; restrict user permissions on system query APIs; deploy behavioral analysis to identify reconnaissance activity chains

threat_vector: Race condition in concurrent requests
exploitation_method: Attacker sends multiple simultaneous requests to exploit timing windows where state validation occurs before state update, allowing duplicate operations (e.g., double-spend, duplicate transactions, unauthorized privilege escalation) before locks acquire or consistency checks complete
detection_pattern: Multiple requests with identical parameters arriving within milliseconds; duplicate transaction IDs in short time windows; audit logs showing state inconsistencies (e.g., inventory going negative, duplicate charges); application logs with 'transaction already processed' errors mixed with successful transactions; database lock timeout spikes; identical user actions logged multiple times with same timestamp or sub-second intervals
mitigation_strategy: Implement distributed locks (Redis, database-level locks) before critical sections; use database transactions with serializable isolation level; add idempotency tokens (unique request IDs validated server-side); enforce request deduplication at application layer; add pessimistic locking on shared resources; implement optimistic locking with version checks; rate-limit per-user concurrent operations; use message queues to serialize state mutations

threat_vector: AppInit DLLs (T1547.010)
exploitation_method: Attacker modifies registry keys (HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Windows\AppInit_DLLs or AppInit_DLLs64) to inject malicious DLLs into processes at startup. The DLL is loaded into user-mode processes early in execution, gaining code execution with process privileges.
detection_pattern: Monitor registry modifications to AppInit_DLLs and AppInit_DLLs64 registry keys; unexpected DLL files referenced in these keys; Event ID 13 (Registry Object added or modified) in Sysmon; suspicious DLLs loaded at process initialization; unsigned or untrusted DLL sources; processes loading DLLs from unusual paths (temp directories, user profiles, network shares)
mitigation_strategy: Disable AppInit_DLLs feature by setting HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Windows\LoadAppInit_DLLs to 0; implement application whitelisting for DLLs; enforce code signing and verify DLL signatures; monitor and restrict write access to AppInit registry keys using GPO; deploy EDR solutions to detect unauthorized DLL injection; regularly audit AppInit_DLLs registry values; apply principle of least privilege to limit process execution contexts

threat_vector: Improper validation of LLM utility timers availability leading to denial of service in Snapdragon platforms
exploitation_method: Attacker sends crafted requests or malformed timer configuration data to LLM utility services without proper availability validation, causing resource exhaustion, infinite loops, or service crashes through timer mismanagement or allocation failures
detection_pattern: Monitor for: repeated timer allocation failures in system logs, anomalous CPU/memory spikes in LLM utility processes, excessive context switches, timer-related error codes (EAGAIN, ENOMEM), service restart loops, watchdog timeout events, and unexplained increases in system call latencies for timer operations
mitigation_strategy: Implement strict input validation for all timer configuration requests including bounds checking and availability verification before allocation; enforce rate limiting on timer requests; add timeout guards on timer operations; maintain timer resource quotas per process; implement health monitoring with automatic service recovery; apply principle of least privilege to timer resource access; regularly audit timer allocation patterns for anomalies

threat_vector: CORS misconfiguration allowing arbitrary origin
exploitation_method: Attacker hosts malicious webpage on external domain and crafts cross-origin requests to vulnerable application. Browser's CORS preflight check passes due to misconfigured Access-Control-Allow-Origin header set to wildcard (*) or reflecting arbitrary origins, allowing JavaScript to read sensitive response data (cookies, tokens, API responses) that would normally be blocked by same-origin policy.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Access-Control-Allow-Origin header set to '*' or reflecting untrusted Origin values in responses; (2) presence of Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true combined with wildcard or reflected origins; (3) OPTIONS preflight requests followed by state-changing requests (POST/PUT/DELETE) from suspicious origins in access logs; (4) unusual cross-origin requests in browser console errors or security event logs showing CORS policy bypasses
mitigation_strategy: Implement strict CORS policy: (1) explicitly whitelist only trusted origins in Access-Control-Allow-Origin header, never use wildcard (*); (2) avoid reflecting user-controlled Origin header values without validation; (3) use Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true only when necessary and paired with specific origins, never with wildcard; (4) restrict Access-Control-Allow-Methods to required HTTP methods only; (5) limit Access-Control-Allow-Headers to necessary headers; (6) implement origin validation logic on server-side; (7) regularly audit CORS configuration and test with browser security tools; (8) apply defense-in-depth with additional authentication mechanisms beyond CORS

threat_vector: Pointer Dereference Vulnerability in GPAC 1.0.1 ShiftMetaOffset.isra
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malformed MP4/media files with invalid metadata offset structures. When GPAC processes these files through ShiftMetaOffset.isra function, unvalidated pointer arithmetic causes dereferencing of invalid memory addresses, triggering segmentation fault or crash.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: GPAC process crashes with SIGSEGV signal; core dumps from GPAC media processing; abnormal memory access patterns in ShiftMetaOffset function; segmentation fault logs when processing specific media files; repeated process termination during file parsing with error codes 139 or 136; valgrind/AddressSanitizer reports of invalid pointer dereferences in GPAC libraries
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade GPAC to version 1.1.0 or later with pointer validation fixes; implement input validation for MP4 metadata offsets before processing; run GPAC in sandboxed environment with memory protection (ASLR, DEP/NX); use fuzzing-based file validation before processing untrusted media; apply resource limits and process isolation to contain DoS impact; monitor file uploads for malformed MP4 headers and reject suspicious structures at ingestion point

threat_vector: Malvertising (T1583.008)
exploitation_method: Attacker purchases ad space on legitimate advertising networks and injects malicious JavaScript or redirects into ad content. When users view the ad, the malicious code executes in their browser context, potentially delivering malware, stealing credentials, or performing drive-by downloads without user interaction.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unexpected JavaScript execution from ad network domains, sudden increase in HTTP 302/307 redirects to unfamiliar domains, anomalous outbound connections from browser processes to non-whitelisted IPs, sudden spike in alert logs from web filter/DLP on ad-serving domains, unusual iframe injections detected in DOM analysis, network traffic to known malware C2 servers originating from ad network requests, browser crash dumps with suspicious memory signatures.
mitigation_strategy: Implement Content Security Policy (CSP) headers to restrict script execution origins; deploy browser isolation for high-risk users; block known malicious ad networks via DNS/proxy filtering; enforce strict ad blocker policies where feasible; require modern browser with auto-update enabled; implement domain reputation scoring and block low-trust ad networks; conduct regular security awareness training on clicking ads; deploy endpoint detection and response (EDR) to catch post-exploitation activity; maintain allowlist of trusted ad networks only.

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Serverless Function Exploitation (T1648)
exploitation_method: Attacker invokes serverless functions (Lambda, Cloud Functions) with malicious payloads to execute code, escalate privileges, access sensitive environment variables, or pivot to other cloud resources. Exploitation leverages misconfigured IAM policies, exposed function endpoints, or supply chain compromises in function dependencies.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unusual function invocation patterns (high frequency, off-hours, from unexpected sources), (2) Function errors with stack traces revealing system information, (3) Environment variable access in logs, (4) Cross-service API calls from function execution role, (5) Abnormal memory/CPU consumption, (6) Failed authentication attempts to function endpoints, (7) Changes to function configuration, code, or IAM execution roles, (8) Presence of reconnaissance tools in function logs (curl, wget, nmap), (9) Exfiltration attempts to external IPs, (10) CloudTrail events showing assume-role from function execution context.
mitigation_strategy: (1) Implement least-privilege IAM execution roles with explicit resource restrictions, (2) Enable function-level authentication and authorization controls, (3) Use VPC endpoints for function-to-AWS service communication, (4) Encrypt environment variables and rotate secrets regularly, (5) Implement code scanning and dependency management in CI/CD pipeline, (6) Enable detailed logging and CloudTrail for all function invocations, (7) Set resource limits (timeout, memory) appropriately, (8) Use Web Application Firewall (WAF) on function endpoints, (9) Implement runtime security monitoring and anomaly detection, (10) Regularly audit and patch function dependencies.

threat_vector: Double free vulnerability in AOD (Always-On Display) module affecting smartphone service integrity
exploitation_method: Attacker triggers memory deallocation twice on the same heap pointer through crafted AOD display requests or malformed sensor input, causing heap corruption that can lead to code execution or denial of service when the corrupted heap is subsequently allocated
detection_pattern: Monitor for: kernel panic logs with heap corruption signatures; repeated SEGFAULT errors from AOD process; abnormal memory addresses in crash dumps; KASAN (Kernel Address Sanitizer) reports indicating 'double-free'; unusual AOD process restarts in rapid succession; heap sanitizer warnings in system logs; unexpected termination of system_server or display service
mitigation_strategy: Implement heap allocator protections (enable KASAN/ASAN in kernel builds); use memory-safe allocators with double-free detection; apply strict input validation for all AOD sensor and display requests; implement bounds checking on memory operations; deploy SELinux policies restricting AOD module capabilities; maintain updated patches for AOD module; use static analysis tools to detect use-after-free patterns in AOD code; implement runtime memory tagging extensions (MTE) on supported devices

threat_vector: Go before 1.16.12 and 1.17.x before 1.17.5 on UNIX - FD 0 mishandling leading to unintended write operations
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts Go application using vulnerable versions where file descriptor 0 (stdin) is incorrectly closed after certain file operations. This causes subsequent write operations to redirect to unintended files or network connections due to FD reassignment, allowing data corruption or unauthorized network communication.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Go application processes with unexpected file descriptor states via lsof/proc filesystem analysis; (2) Unusual file modifications to unexpected locations by Go processes; (3) Network connections initiated by Go processes without corresponding application logic; (4) File descriptor 0 being assigned to files other than stdin in process tracing; (5) Go version detection via 'go version' or binary analysis showing versions <1.16.12 or 1.17.0-1.17.4
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade Go runtime to 1.16.12, 1.17.5 or later immediately. Implement: (1) Version pinning in build pipelines with automated scanning for vulnerable Go versions; (2) Runtime process monitoring to restrict file descriptor manipulation; (3) File integrity monitoring (FIM) on critical files; (4) Network egress filtering to detect anomalous connections; (5) Code review to identify vulnerable file handling patterns; (6) Container image scanning for vulnerable Go versions in supply chain

threat_vector: Archive via Custom Method
exploitation_method: Attacker uses custom scripts or non-standard compression tools (7-Zip, RAR, custom Python/PowerShell scripts) to archive sensitive data before exfiltration, bypassing detection rules tuned for common archive formats like ZIP or default compression utilities
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unusual process execution (custom compression tools), high entropy file writes, unexpected file I/O patterns to archive creation, process command-line arguments containing compression parameters, child processes spawned from file managers/explorers performing archive operations, network connections from archiving processes, deletion of source files post-archiving, unusual file extensions created (.7z, .rar, .tar.gz variants)
mitigation_strategy: Implement application allowlisting to restrict compression tools to approved versions, deploy endpoint detection and response (EDR) with behavioral analysis for bulk file operations, enforce file integrity monitoring on sensitive data directories, restrict access to compression utilities via Group Policy or endpoint controls, monitor and log all compression tool execution with command-line auditing, segment and encrypt sensitive data at rest, implement data loss prevention (DLP) rules to block transfer of large archives, conduct regular audits of installed software and user capabilities

threat_vector: Reflected XSS in Github Read Me Stats renderError function (commit 3c7220e4f7144f6cb068fd433c774f6db47ccb95)
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious URL with unsanitized user input passed to renderError function, which reflects the payload in HTTP response without encoding. When victim clicks link or visits crafted URL, JavaScript executes in their browser context with access to session tokens and credentials.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) HTTP requests with suspicious characters in query parameters (script tags, event handlers, encoded payloads); (2) Response content containing unescaped user input reflected verbatim; (3) Browser console errors indicating inline script execution; (4) CSP violation reports; (5) Access logs showing URLs with XSS payloads (e.g., %3Cscript%3E, onerror=, javascript:); (6) WAF alerts on renderError endpoint with special characters
mitigation_strategy: Implement output encoding on all user inputs in renderError function using context-appropriate encoding (HTML entity encoding for HTML context); deploy Content Security Policy (CSP) with strict-dynamic and nonce-based inline script restrictions; apply input validation with allowlist approach; use modern templating engines with auto-escaping enabled; add httpOnly and Secure flags to cookies; implement subresource integrity (SRI) for external resources; conduct code review of renderError and similar error handling functions; update to patched version and deploy security headers (X-XSS-Protection, X-Content-Type-Options)

threat_vector: Accu-Time Systems MAXIMUS 1.0 telnet service remote buffer overflow
exploitation_method: Attacker sends oversized input strings to telnet service port (default 23) exceeding buffer boundaries in command parsing functions, corrupting stack memory and causing denial of service or potential code execution
detection_pattern: Monitor for: telnet connection attempts with abnormally large payloads (>1024 bytes in single command), unexpected telnet service crashes with segmentation fault errors, repeated connection attempts followed by immediate disconnections, anomalous process termination logs for telnet daemon, IDS alerts for buffer overflow signatures on port 23
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade MAXIMUS to patched version; disable telnet service and migrate to SSH with key-based authentication; implement network-level access controls restricting telnet port 23 to authorized administrative networks only; deploy rate limiting on connection attempts; monitor telnet service process for crashes and configure automatic restart with alerting; implement input validation and length checks at firewall/IDS level

threat_vector: Timing Side-Channel Attack on Smartphone Cryptographic Operations
exploitation_method: Attacker measures execution time variations in cryptographic operations (e.g., key comparison, encryption, authentication) to infer sensitive information like cryptographic keys or authentication tokens through statistical analysis of timing differences in CPU cycles
detection_pattern: Monitor for: abnormal process CPU cycle patterns, repeated rapid cryptographic operation calls from non-privileged processes, statistical clustering of operation completion times, unusually frequent timing measurements via performance counters, repeated authentication attempts with microsecond-precision timing, anomalous access to performance monitoring APIs (perf, PAPI)
mitigation_strategy: Implement constant-time cryptographic implementations for all sensitive operations; use compiler flags to prevent timing-dependent optimizations; add random delays (blinding/jitter) to cryptographic operations; isolate cryptographic processing in secure enclaves (TEE); restrict access to high-resolution timers for unprivileged processes; perform regular cryptographic code audits for timing vulnerabilities; update to patched OS versions with timing-safe library implementations

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Startup Items (T1547.001)
exploitation_method: Attacker modifies startup folders, registry Run keys (HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run), or creates scheduled tasks to execute malicious code upon system boot or user login, achieving persistence.
detection_pattern: Monitor creation/modification of files in %ProgramData%\Microsoft\Windows\Start Menu\Programs\Startup and %AppData%\Microsoft\Windows\Start Menu\Programs\Startup; track Registry modifications to Run, RunOnce, RunServices keys; analyze Sysmon Event ID 13 (Registry Set) for suspicious HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run entries; correlate unusual process spawning at startup with these registry changes.
