detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) path parameters containing ../ or ..\, (2) URL-encoded traversal sequences (%2e%2e/, %252e%252e/), (3) unusual file access patterns in logs (access to /etc/, /config/, parent directories), (4) repeated 404 errors followed by 200s on non-standard paths, (5) file inclusion attempts in web server logs with depth indicators, (6) access to files with extensions like .conf, .env, .pem, .key, .sql outside web root
mitigation_strategy: Implement: (1) strict input validation - reject or sanitize any path parameter containing traversal sequences, (2) use allowlist validation for file names/paths, (3) canonicalize file paths and verify resolved path remains within intended directory, (4) run application with minimal file permissions (principle of least privilege), (5) disable directory listing, (6) store sensitive files outside web-accessible directories, (7) use security headers (X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff), (8) implement Web Application Firewall (WAF) rules to block traversal patterns, (9) regular security scanning and file integrity monitoring

threat_vector: MediaWiki Special:CheckUserLog XSS vulnerability via date parameter mishandling (CVE-2021-44855)
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious date parameter in Special:CheckUserLog URL containing JavaScript payload. Due to insufficient sanitization of date input before rendering in HTML context, the payload executes in victim's browser with CheckUser privileges.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) HTTP requests to Special:CheckUserLog with unusual date parameters containing script tags or event handlers; (2) Log entries showing date parameters with lengths exceeding normal values; (3) Database queries logging CheckUserLog access with special characters in date fields; (4) Browser console errors or security warnings related to inline script execution on CheckUserLog page
mitigation_strategy: Immediately upgrade to MediaWiki 1.35.5, 1.36.3, or 1.37.1+. Implement strict input validation on date parameters using allowlist of valid date formats. Apply HTML entity encoding to all date values before output. Deploy Content Security Policy (CSP) headers to prevent inline script execution. Restrict access to Special:CheckUserLog to trusted CheckUser accounts only via role-based access controls.

threat_vector: Out of bounds memory access due to improper validation of frame count in audio playback processing
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious audio file or stream with invalid frame count field that exceeds allocated buffer size, causing memory access beyond buffer boundaries during playback processing
detection_pattern: Memory access violations, segmentation faults, unexpected process termination, heap corruption indicators, fuzzing results showing crash on malformed frame count values, static analysis detecting missing bounds checks on frame indexing
mitigation_strategy: Implement strict validation of frame count parameter before processing: verify frame count against maximum buffer size, use safe integer arithmetic to prevent overflow, add bounds checking on all frame array accesses, employ memory protection mechanisms (AddressSanitizer/ASAN), conduct fuzzing on audio input parsers, use safe APIs that enforce bounds, implement input whitelisting for frame count ranges

threat_vector: Discourse whisper participant disclosure via composer user suggestions
exploitation_method: When composing a message in a Discourse topic, the auto-complete user suggestion feature displays all participants in whisper conversations, including those whose participation should be confidential. An attacker with basic forum access can trigger suggestions to enumerate whisper participants without direct access to those conversations.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unusual frequency of composer suggestion API requests, (2) Log entries showing user_suggestions queries from low-privilege accounts, (3) Autocomplete feature returning whisper participant names in suggestion results, (4) Access patterns where users request suggestions while composing but don't post messages, (5) Database queries selecting whisper-related user data during suggestion generation
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade to patched Discourse version; implement server-side filtering in user suggestion endpoint to exclude whisper participants from autocomplete results for users without whisper access; add capability checks before populating suggestions to verify user has permission to view conversation participants; audit composer suggestion API logs for anomalous request patterns; restrict autocomplete data scope based on user permissions model

threat_vector: Modem EMM (Enterprise Mobility Management) missing data encryption leading to information disclosure
exploitation_method: Attacker intercepts unencrypted network traffic between modem and EMM server, or accesses locally stored unencrypted sensitive data (credentials, device identifiers, configuration) through packet sniffing, man-in-the-middle attack, or direct file system access on compromised network segments
detection_pattern: Network monitoring: unencrypted HTTP/non-TLS protocols for EMM data transmission, baseline deviation in cleartext credential patterns in logs; endpoint logging: access to EMM configuration files without encryption markers, authentication tokens in plaintext in traffic captures or device memory; SIEM alerts on unusual data exfiltration patterns matching EMM sensitive fields
mitigation_strategy: Enforce mandatory TLS/SSL 1.3+ encryption for all EMM-modem communications; implement full-disk encryption and encrypted data-at-rest for stored credentials; deploy network segmentation isolating EMM traffic; enforce certificate pinning for modem-EMM endpoints; implement DLP (Data Loss Prevention) rules to detect plaintext credential transmission; mandate secure credential storage using hardware-backed key management; conduct regular encryption audit assessments

threat_vector: Service logic vulnerability in smartphone WLAN stack causing denial of service
exploitation_method: Attacker sends malformed or malicious WLAN management frames (beacon, probe response, or association frames) that trigger improper state handling in the smartphone's WLAN service logic, causing the radio interface to crash, hang, or enter an infinite loop, resulting in WLAN unavailability
detection_pattern: Monitor for: frequent WLAN radio resets/crashes, repeated 'WLAN service stopped unexpectedly' logs, abnormal management frame sequences from single MAC address, correlation of specific frame patterns with connectivity loss events, syslog entries showing kernel panics in wireless driver, sudden increase in deauthentication/disassociation frames from single AP, device unable to reconnect to previously accessible networks
mitigation_strategy: Apply security patches/firmware updates to WLAN drivers; implement frame validation and bounds checking in WLAN state machine; add rate limiting on management frames from unknown sources; deploy intrusion detection on network perimeter to identify attack patterns; configure devices to ignore malformed frames; enable WLAN connection monitoring with automatic recovery mechanisms; segment networks to limit broadcast of malicious frames; maintain updated device inventory to ensure patches are deployed

threat_vector: Memory exhaustion vulnerability in libming 0.4.8 cws2fws function via malformed SWF file
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts a malicious SWF file with excessive uncompressed size declarations that cause the cws2fws decompression function to allocate unbounded memory when processing the file, leading to heap exhaustion and process termination
detection_pattern: Monitor for: sudden spike in memory allocation requests from libming processes, process memory usage exceeding expected thresholds for SWF file size, malloc/brk system calls with unusually large allocation sizes, process crashes with out-of-memory errors, unusual CPU spikes during SWF processing, abnormal heap growth patterns in debugger output
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade libming to version 0.4.9 or later with memory bounds checking; implement input validation to verify SWF file headers and size declarations before processing; set resource limits (ulimit -v) on processes handling SWF files; use memory sandbox/containers with defined heap limits; implement timeout mechanisms for SWF conversion operations; deploy WAF rules to reject SWF files exceeding size thresholds; monitor and alert on memory exhaustion patterns in application logs

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Credentials in Registry
exploitation_method: Attacker queries Windows Registry (e.g., HKLM, HKCU) to extract plaintext or encoded credentials stored by applications, services, or system components. Common targets include RDP connection strings, VPN profiles, SQL Server credentials, and cached authentication tokens in registry hives.
detection_pattern: Monitor registry access events (Event ID 4657, 4656) for suspicious queries to HKLM\Software, HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\PasswordLess\Device, or credential manager paths. Alert on reg.exe/regedit.exe execution with read flags targeting sensitive hives. Detect unusual reg query commands via process monitoring and command line auditing.
mitigation_strategy: Implement credential storage best practices: avoid storing credentials in registry; use Windows Credential Manager APIs, DPAPI encryption, or Azure Key Vault. Enable Registry auditing and restrict registry access via ACLs. Deploy application-level secrets management. Use credential guard to isolate LSASS process. Enforce code signing for registry-querying tools. Conduct regular registry audits for hardcoded secrets.

threat_vector: radare2 Out-of-bounds Read vulnerability
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious binary file with specially crafted headers or sections that cause radare2's parsing functions to read memory beyond allocated buffer boundaries when analyzing the file structure, potentially exposing sensitive information or causing denial of service
detection_pattern: Monitor for: radare2 process crashes with segmentation faults, abnormal memory access patterns in radare2 process, valgrind/ASAN alerts showing out-of-bounds reads, analysis of suspiciously formatted ELF/PE/Mach-O headers with invalid section sizes or offsets, radare2 consuming excessive memory during file analysis
mitigation_strategy: Keep radare2 updated to latest patched version, implement fuzzing-resistant file validation before passing to radare2, run radare2 in sandboxed environment with memory protection, use AddressSanitizer during development, implement strict input validation for binary file headers, set resource limits (memory, CPU) on radare2 processes, consider using alternative analysis tools with proven memory safety for untrusted binaries

threat_vector: WordPress PHP sanitization vulnerability in core class allowing unsanitized user input processing
exploitation_method: Attacker submits malicious payload through WordPress input fields (comments, posts, forms, admin panels) that lacks proper sanitization, allowing SQL injection, XSS, or command injection depending on context and database queries
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unusual SQL queries with union/select statements in logs, script tags or javascript: protocols in database content, repeated failed sanitization bypass attempts, suspicious POST requests with encoded payloads, error_log entries showing unexpected PHP execution, database query logs showing parameter tampering
mitigation_strategy: 1) Update WordPress to latest version immediately, 2) Use WordPress security plugins with input validation rules, 3) Implement Web Application Firewall (WAF) rules blocking known payload patterns, 4) Configure prepared statements/parameterized queries in custom code, 5) Apply principle of least privilege to database user accounts, 6) Enable WordPress security headers (CSP, X-Frame-Options), 7) Conduct code audit of custom themes/plugins, 8) Implement output escaping functions (esc_sql, esc_attr, esc_html) throughout codebase

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Downgrade Attack (T1562.010)
exploitation_method: Attacker intercepts protocol negotiation to force use of weaker cryptographic algorithms, older protocol versions (SSL 3.0, TLS 1.0), or disabled security features. Achieved through man-in-the-middle positioning or by manipulating client/server hello messages during handshake.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: TLS version downgrades in connection logs (negotiated_protocol < client_supported_version), unexpected use of deprecated ciphers (DES, MD5), ClientHello messages with inconsistent version fields, repeated failed handshakes followed by successful connection with lower protocol version, anomalous fallback SCSV signals (TLS_FALLBACK_SCSV), network traffic using unencrypted protocols after encrypted connection attempts.
mitigation_strategy: Disable legacy protocols (SSLv3, TLS 1.0/1.1) at OS/application level. Enforce minimum TLS 1.2 or higher. Implement strict cipher suite allowlists excluding weak algorithms. Deploy TLS Fallback SCSV support to detect downgrade attacks. Use HSTS headers to prevent HTTP downgrade. Employ certificate pinning for critical connections. Monitor and alert on any negotiation of deprecated protocols. Conduct regular cipher audits and disable any non-FIPS approved algorithms.

threat_vector: wolfSSL wolfMQTT 1.9 heap-based buffer overflow in MqttDecode_Publish
exploitation_method: Attacker sends a specially crafted MQTT PUBLISH packet with malformed variable-length encoding or topic name that causes 4-byte heap buffer overflow when MqttDecode_Publish processes the packet through MqttClient_DecodePacket and MqttClient_HandlePacket functions, potentially enabling arbitrary code execution or denial of service
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) MQTT clients abruptly disconnecting or crashing with memory corruption errors; (2) Valgrind/AddressSanitizer reports of heap-buffer-overflow in libwolfmqtt.so during PUBLISH packet handling; (3) Segmentation faults or SIGABRT signals in wolfMQTT processes; (4) Unusual MQTT PUBLISH packets with topic lengths exceeding protocol specifications or malformed variable-length integer encoding; (5) Heap canary corruption detected in memory dumps; (6) Core dumps showing corrupted heap metadata near MqttDecode_Publish stack frames
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade wolfSSL wolfMQTT to version 1.10 or later containing the buffer overflow fix; implement strict input validation for MQTT PUBLISH packet fields before processing; use AddressSanitizer (ASan) or similar runtime memory protection during testing and deployment; enable heap protections (ASLR, DEP/NX); run MQTT clients with least privilege and memory limits; implement network segmentation to restrict MQTT broker access; deploy WAF/NIDS rules to identify malformed MQTT packets; establish version control requiring patched wolfMQTT versions in dependency management

threat_vector: Formpipe Lasernet <9.13.3 Client Web Services File Inclusion Vulnerability
exploitation_method: Attacker submits crafted requests to Client Web Services endpoints with path traversal sequences (../ or encoded variants) or absolute file paths in parameters intended for local file operations, bypassing input validation to access arbitrary files on the server filesystem
detection_pattern: HTTP requests containing encoded traversal sequences (%2e%2e, %252e), path traversal attempts (../, ..\), or suspicious file paths in Client Web Services parameters; server logs showing file access errors or successful reads of sensitive files like /etc/passwd, configuration files, or source code; 403/400 errors followed by successful file content responses; POST/GET requests to */clientweb/* endpoints with unusual path parameters
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade Formpipe Lasernet to version 9.13.3 or later; implement strict input validation and whitelisting for all file path parameters in Client Web Services; use filesystem access controls to restrict web service process privileges; implement Web Application Firewall (WAF) rules to block path traversal patterns; disable Client Web Services if not required; enforce authentication and authorization checks for all file operations; implement request logging and alerting for suspicious file access patterns

threat_vector: Pointer Dereference in GPAC 1.0.1 shift_chunk_offsets.part function leading to Denial of Service
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts a malformed MP4 or similar media file with invalid chunk offset data that causes the shift_chunk_offsets.part function to dereference a null or invalid pointer when processing chunk offset values, resulting in application crash or memory access violation
detection_pattern: Monitor for: segmentation faults or access violations in GPAC processes; abnormal termination of media processing threads; error logs containing 'shift_chunk_offsets' or null pointer dereference exceptions; unexpected crashes when parsing specific media files; core dumps referencing the vulnerable function; application-level exceptions during media file parsing or chunk processing
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade GPAC to version 1.0.2 or later where this vulnerability is patched; implement strict input validation for media file chunk offset structures before processing; add pointer null-checks and bounds validation in the shift_chunk_offsets.part function; use memory safety tools (AddressSanitizer, Valgrind) during testing; implement sandboxing for media file processing; restrict GPAC processing to trusted input sources only; monitor GPAC process health and implement automatic restart mechanisms for crash detection

threat_vector: Stored XSS in Bludit 3.13.1 About Plugin login panel
exploitation_method: Attacker injects malicious JavaScript into the About Plugin settings accessible through the login panel. The payload is stored in the database and executed when administrators or users view the affected plugin page, allowing session hijacking, credential theft, or administrative account compromise.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unusual script tags or event handlers in About Plugin database entries; (2) POST requests to plugin configuration endpoints with script payloads; (3) Suspicious JavaScript execution in browser console during plugin page loads; (4) Web Application Firewall alerts on script injection attempts to /admin/ paths; (5) Unexpected DOM modifications in login/plugin panel HTML; (6) Log entries showing discrepancies between stored plugin content and rendered output
mitigation_strategy: Immediately upgrade Bludit to version 3.14.0 or later. Implement: (1) input validation and sanitization on all plugin configuration fields; (2) output encoding of all user-supplied data; (3) Content Security Policy headers restricting inline scripts; (4) Regular security audits of plugin functionality; (5) Principle of least privilege for plugin configuration access; (6) Database queries to identify and remove existing malicious payloads from About Plugin settings

threat_vector: MyHuawei-App Sensitive Information Exposure to Unauthorized Actor
exploitation_method: Attacker intercepts or accesses unencrypted sensitive data (credentials, tokens, personal information, API keys) transmitted by MyHuawei-App through insecure channels, weak encryption, improper storage, or by exploiting insufficient access controls on exposed endpoints or local storage.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unencrypted HTTP traffic from MyHuawei-App, base64-encoded credentials in network packets, API tokens in cleartext logs, sensitive data in app cache/SharedPreferences, unusual data access patterns, failed authentication attempts followed by successful access with leaked credentials, abnormal API calls using captured tokens, and review application logs for unauthorized data retrieval requests.
mitigation_strategy: Enforce TLS 1.2+ for all data transmission, implement certificate pinning, encrypt sensitive data at rest using platform-provided secure storage (Android Keystore/iOS Keychain), implement proper access controls and authentication mechanisms, use secure API authentication (OAuth 2.0), conduct regular security code reviews, perform penetration testing focusing on data exposure vectors, implement data minimization principles, enable secure logging without logging sensitive information, deploy network monitoring to detect exfiltration, and promptly apply security patches when available.

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Network Sniffing (T1040)
exploitation_method: Attacker captures unencrypted network traffic using packet sniffing tools (tcpdump, Wireshark) on compromised host or via ARP spoofing/MITM position to intercept credentials, session tokens, and sensitive data transmitted over the network in plaintext protocols (HTTP, FTP, Telnet, SMTP).
detection_pattern: Monitor for: promiscuous mode enabled on network interfaces (ifconfig/ip link output), suspicious packet capture tools (tcpdump, tshark processes), unusual network interface state changes, DHCP requests from unexpected hosts, excessive ARP traffic or gratuitous ARP packets, network monitoring tools like Ettercap or mitmproxy running, unexpected listening sockets on high-numbered ports, and NetFlow/sFlow data showing unusual traffic patterns or packet-in indicators.
mitigation_strategy: Enforce encrypted protocols (HTTPS, SSH, SFTP, TLS/SSL) for all sensitive communications, implement network segmentation and VLANs to isolate sensitive traffic, deploy network-based IDS/IPS to detect sniffing signatures, disable promiscuous mode via host-based firewall rules, use IPsec or VPN for transport-layer encryption, implement MAC filtering and port security on switches to prevent spoofing, monitor for rogue DHCP servers and implement DHCP snooping, conduct regular security audits to identify cleartext protocol usage, and apply host-based controls restricting packet capture capabilities to authorized administrators only.

threat_vector: BIOS firmware vulnerability allowing empty Platform Authorization value, enabling TPM bricking and system DoS
exploitation_method: Attacker exploits uninitialized or improperly validated Platform Authorization field in BIOS to send malformed TPM commands that either permanently corrupt TPM state (bricking) or trigger resource exhaustion leading to temporary denial of service
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) TPM error codes 0x01 (TPM_E_AUTHFAIL) and 0x26 (TPM_E_DISABLED) in system logs; (2) repeated failed TPM_SetCapability or TPM_PhysicalDisable commands; (3) unexpected TPM firmware state transitions; (4) Event Viewer entries showing 'Trusted Platform Module' warnings or critical errors; (5) TCG event log anomalies indicating unauthorized authorization changes; (6) system hangs correlating with TPM access attempts
mitigation_strategy: Apply BIOS security patches from vendor immediately; validate Platform Authorization value during POST and reject empty/invalid values; implement secure boot attestation checks; enable Hardware Security Module (HSM) logging; configure BIOS to lock TPM settings after initialization; deploy endpoint detection responding to TPM failures by quarantining affected systems; maintain BIOS version inventory and enforce mandatory firmware updates; consider firmware rollback protection to prevent regression to vulnerable versions

threat_vector: Implicit Intent Hijacking in ActivityMetricsLogger (pre-SMR Jan-2022 Release 1)
exploitation_method: Attacker creates a malicious application with intent filters matching implicit intents sent by ActivityMetricsLogger. The vulnerable component fails to specify explicit component names, allowing the attacker's app to intercept intent broadcasts and receive sensitive running application data including process information, activity names, and system metrics.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unexpected applications receiving PACKAGE_USAGE_STATS intents; manifest files with overly broad intent filters matching system component patterns; multiple apps declaring identical intent filters; audit logs showing intent resolution to non-system packages; ActivityMetricsLogger sending implicit intents in logcat; permission grants for PACKAGE_USAGE_STATS to third-party applications without user interaction
mitigation_strategy: Update to SMR Jan-2022 Release 1 or later; enforce explicit intent usage in ActivityMetricsLogger by specifying component names; restrict PACKAGE_USAGE_STATS permission through SELinux policies; implement intent signature verification; audit and remove overly permissive intent filters from system components; disable implicit intents for sensitive system services; use runtime permission checks before sharing application metrics data

threat_vector: XML external entity injection (XXE)
exploitation_method: Attacker injects malicious XML DOCTYPE declarations with external entity references into XML input. When the XML parser processes the input without proper validation, it resolves these entities, allowing attackers to read local files, perform SSRF attacks, or cause denial of service through billion laughs attacks.
detection_pattern: Monitor for DOCTYPE declarations in XML input, particularly those containing SYSTEM/PUBLIC keywords pointing to file:// or http:// URIs. Look for patterns like <!DOCTYPE foo [<!ENTITY xxe SYSTEM 'file:///etc/passwd'>]>. Detect unusual parser errors, timeouts, or excessive memory consumption. Track requests to internal services or file access attempts from XML parsing contexts.
mitigation_strategy: Disable XML external entity processing in all XML parsers by setting appropriate flags (e.g., XMLConstants.ACCESS_EXTERNAL_DTD and ACCESS_EXTERNAL_SCHEMA to empty string in Java). Implement strict XML schema validation. Use allowlisting for entity definitions. Disable DOCTYPE declarations entirely if not required. Apply input validation before XML parsing. Use modern parsing libraries with secure defaults. Implement network segmentation to restrict XML parser access to internal resources.

threat_vector: HarfBuzz 2.9.0 out-of-bounds write in hb_bit_set_invertible_t::set
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malformed font file or glyph data that triggers memory allocation with insufficient bounds checking in HarfBuzz's bit set operations. When hb_set_copy or hb_sparseset_t operations process the crafted input, the invertible bit set write operation exceeds allocated buffer boundaries, enabling heap corruption.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) application crashes with SIGSEGV in libharfbuzz.so during font rendering, (2) heap corruption indicators in memory sanitizer logs, (3) abnormal memory access patterns near hb_bit_set_invertible_t::set in debugger/valgrind output, (4) process termination while loading untrusted font files, (5) upstream HarfBuzz CVE-2022-33403 related alerts
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade HarfBuzz to version 2.9.1 or later containing the bounds check fix. Implement input validation for font files before processing: reject fonts with suspicious bit set structures. Deploy memory protection mechanisms (ASLR, DEP/NX). Run font rendering in sandboxed processes with restricted privileges. Use AddressSanitizer or MemorySanitizer in development to catch heap overflow attempts.

threat_vector: MDB Tools 0.9.2 stack-based buffer overflow in mdb_numeric_to_string function
exploitation_method: Attacker provides specially crafted numeric data that exceeds buffer boundaries when processed by mdb_numeric_to_string during data transfer or binding operations. The overflow occurs in the call chain: _mdb_attempt_bind/mdb_xfer_bound_data → mdb_numeric_to_string, allowing stack memory corruption and potential code execution.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) crashes or segmentation faults in mdbtools processes with stack traces referencing mdb_numeric_to_string; (2) unexpected process termination with signal 11 (SIGSEGV); (3) abnormal data values in MDB database numeric fields with excessive length; (4) core dumps showing corrupted stack frames near 0x7ffd6e029ee0 memory region; (5) canary corruption detection if stack protection enabled; (6) ASLR bypass attempts preceding mdbtools execution
mitigation_strategy: Immediately upgrade MDB Tools to version 0.9.3 or later containing the buffer overflow fix. Implement input validation for numeric field lengths before processing. Deploy stack protection mechanisms (stack canaries, DEP/NX, ASLR). Run mdbtools processes with minimal privileges and in sandboxed environments. Apply strict memory access controls. Monitor and log all MDB file access operations. Consider disabling mdbtools if not actively required and substitute with alternative data access methods.

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1098.003 - Additional Cloud Roles
exploitation_method: Attacker with compromised credentials or initial access adds new IAM roles to their own cloud account user or creates new users with elevated permissions. This enables persistent access and privilege escalation while maintaining additional backdoors for lateral movement.
detection_pattern: CloudTrail/audit logs showing: CreateRole, AttachUserPolicy, CreateAccessKey with unusual accounts; unexpected IAM permission changes; new administrative roles created during off-hours; users gaining permissions they didn't previously have; rapid successive role/policy attachment events; anomalous AssumeRole activities from non-standard principals
mitigation_strategy: Implement IAM policy conditions restricting role creation/attachment to specific service roles; enable MFA for all IAM modifications; use resource-based policies to limit cross-account access; enforce principle of least privilege with permission boundaries; monitor and alert on all IAM permission changes in real-time; regularly audit active roles and access keys; implement approval workflows for privilege escalation; use temporary credentials instead of long-lived access keys; enable CloudTrail logging with integrity validation

threat_vector: Stack-based buffer overflow in image_load_bmp() function in HTMLDOC <= 1.9.13 via crafted BMP file
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts a malicious BMP file with oversized image dimensions or header fields that exceed allocated stack buffer size in image_load_bmp(). When HTMLDOC processes HTML containing a link to this BMP, the function writes beyond buffer boundaries, overwriting the stack frame and return address, allowing arbitrary code execution with the privileges of the HTMLDOC process.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) HTMLDOC process crashes with segmentation faults when processing specific BMP files; (2) abnormal memory access patterns detected by Address Sanitizer or Valgrind; (3) BMP files with unusually large dimension values (width/height > reasonable thresholds); (4) unexpected child process spawning from HTMLDOC; (5) file access logs showing HTMLDOC reading from suspicious external BMP sources; (6) core dump generation with stack corruption indicators
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade HTMLDOC to version > 1.9.13 which includes bounds checking fixes. Implement: (1) input validation to reject BMP files with unrealistic dimensions; (2) process sandboxing/containerization to limit HTMLDOC privileges; (3) disable automatic image loading from untrusted sources; (4) deploy stack canaries and ASLR at OS level; (5) use static analysis tools to identify buffer overflow vulnerabilities; (6) restrict HTMLDOC file access permissions via SELinux/AppArmor; (7) monitor and alert on suspicious BMP file characteristics before processing

threat_vector: Double free vulnerability in smartphone kernel memory management
exploitation_method: Attacker triggers code path that attempts to free the same memory allocation twice, corrupting heap metadata and potentially gaining arbitrary code execution with kernel privileges through heap exploitation techniques
detection_pattern: Kernel panic logs with heap corruption signatures; Address Sanitizer (ASAN) or Memory Tagging Extension (MTE) reports of double-free; unexpected kernel crashes with heap-related stack traces; syslog entries showing memory allocator warnings; abnormal process termination with segmentation faults correlating with specific application behavior
mitigation_strategy: Implement heap hardening: enable MTE (ARM Memory Tagging) or similar hardware memory protection; use safe allocator libraries with double-free detection; apply pointer encryption; implement strict bounds checking; enable kernel Address Space Layout Randomization (ASLR); deploy Control Flow Integrity (CFI); regularly patch memory management code; use static analysis tools to identify potential double-free conditions; implement rate limiting on memory allocation failures

threat_vector: HwNearbyMain module - Improper Handling of Exceptional Conditions leading to message leak
exploitation_method: Attacker triggers an unhandled exception in the HwNearbyMain module by sending malformed nearby discovery packets or triggering specific runtime conditions. The exception handler fails to sanitize error messages before logging or returning them, allowing sensitive information (device details, identifiers, or communication metadata) to be exposed.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unusual exception stack traces in HwNearbyMain logs containing sensitive data, (2) Repeated malformed nearby discovery packets followed by verbose error output, (3) Abnormal message content in exception handlers referencing device tokens or IDs, (4) Correlate crash logs with information disclosure artifacts, (5) Watch for patterns where error messages contain data that should not be exposed in debug output
mitigation_strategy: Implement proper exception handling in HwNearbyMain: (1) Catch specific exceptions rather than generic handlers, (2) Sanitize all error messages before logging - strip sensitive data like device IDs, tokens, or addresses, (3) Use generic error responses for external-facing APIs, (4) Apply input validation to nearby discovery messages before processing, (5) Implement rate limiting on exception generation, (6) Review and harden exception handlers in proximity communication modules, (7) Apply principle of least privilege to module permissions, (8) Enable code obfuscation for sensitive error paths

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1053.007 - Scheduled Task/Job: Container Orchestration Job
exploitation_method: Attacker creates or modifies scheduled jobs/cronjobs in container orchestration platforms (Kubernetes, Docker Swarm) to execute malicious payloads at specified intervals. Typically involves deploying CronJob objects with malicious container images or commands that establish persistence or exfiltrate data.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unusual CronJob/scheduled job creation events in API audit logs; (2) CronJob specs with suspicious image registries or pull policies set to 'Always'; (3) Jobs spawning with elevated privileges or host mounts; (4) Scheduled tasks executed by service accounts with excessive RBAC permissions; (5) Cronjobs modifying system files or network configurations; (6) Failed job executions followed by rapid retries; (7) CronJobs scheduled during off-hours with non-standard frequencies
mitigation_strategy: (1) Implement strict RBAC policies limiting who can create/modify CronJobs and scheduled jobs; (2) Use admission controllers (ValidatingWebhookConfiguration) to restrict allowed image registries and enforce image signing; (3) Enable audit logging for all job creation/modification events; (4) Implement resource quotas to prevent resource exhaustion via job scheduling; (5) Use Pod Security Policies/Standards to prevent privileged job containers; (6) Regular audits of existing scheduled jobs and their purposes; (7) Implement network policies restricting job egress; (8) Monitor job execution patterns and alert on anomalous scheduling

threat_vector: Integer overflow in page alignment interface due to insufficient address and size validation in Snapdragon platform components
exploitation_method: Attacker supplies oversized or specially crafted address/size parameters to alignment functions, causing integer overflow that results in incorrect memory calculations, leading to out-of-bounds access, privilege escalation, or code execution
detection_pattern: Monitor for: alignment function calls with size parameters near UINT_MAX or SIZE_MAX boundaries, mismatched pre/post alignment calculations in kernel logs, page allocation failures followed by memory access violations, syscall audits showing invalid memory ranges passed to alignment APIs, CPU exceptions (page faults, segmentation faults) correlating with alignment operations
mitigation_strategy: Implement strict input validation before all alignment operations: enforce maximum size limits, validate address ranges are within process virtual address space, use safe integer arithmetic libraries to detect overflow conditions, add pre-calculation overflow checks before performing alignment math, enable CONFIG_HARDENED_USERCOPY and similar kernel hardening options, apply vendor security patches for Snapdragon firmware, implement Address Space Layout Randomization (ASLR), use memory tagging extensions (MTE) where available on ARM platforms

threat_vector: Buffer over-read in libMtkOmxGsmDec due to incorrect bounds check leading to information disclosure
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malformed GSM audio stream with invalid frame parameters that bypass bounds validation in the decoder library, causing the decoder to read beyond allocated buffer boundaries and leak adjacent memory contents to the application layer
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) libMtkOmxGsmDec crashes or segfaults with SIGSEGV on malformed input, (2) Unexpected memory access patterns in decoder logs, (3) OMX component error codes indicating buffer overrun (OMX_ErrorBadParameter), (4) System logs showing out-of-bounds memory reads via AddressSanitizer/valgrind, (5) Audio processing applications accessing memory regions outside declared GSM frame buffers
mitigation_strategy: Apply vendor security patch for libMtkOmxGsmDec to add proper bounds validation before all buffer reads; implement input validation that verifies frame size, offset parameters, and allocated buffer size before decoding; use AddressSanitizer at compile-time for memory safety checks; restrict libMtkOmxGsmDec execution context with SELinux policies limiting memory access; deploy fuzzing on GSM stream input to catch similar boundary violations; update to patched Android/MediaTek firmware versions

threat_vector: CodeIgniter 4 Unsafe Deserialization in old() Function
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious serialized PHP objects and injects them through the old() function parameter. When unserialize() processes untrusted input, it instantiates arbitrary classes with __wakeup() or __destruct() magic methods, leading to remote code execution through the autoloader chain.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) base64_decode() or unserialize() calls with user-controlled input in logs; (2) HTTP requests with serialized object patterns (O:class_name:length); (3) Unusual class instantiation in debug logs; (4) File write/execution events immediately following deserialize operations; (5) Exception logs showing 'Class not found' or autoloader errors with suspicious namespaces
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade CodeIgniter to patched version (4.1.5+). Implement input validation: whitelist allowed data types before deserialization, avoid unserialize() on user input (use json_decode instead), disable dangerous PHP functions in php.ini (disable_classes/disable_functions), apply strict object filtering, implement request signing/HMAC validation for serialized data, and deploy Web Application Firewall rules to block requests containing serialized object patterns in parameters.

threat_vector: Keylogging (T1056.001)
exploitation_method: Attacker installs software or kernel-level driver that captures keyboard input and stores or transmits it to remote server. Methods include user-mode API hooking, DirectInput interception, or kernel-mode driver installation.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unsigned drivers or drivers from untrusted publishers; unusual API hooks on keyboard input functions (SetWindowsHookEx, GetAsyncKeyState); processes with unusual privileges accessing HID devices; network connections from suspicious processes; elevated file I/O activity in temp directories; anomalous registry modifications to HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Services; EDR alerts on process injection or API hooking; unexpected system calls related to input capture.
mitigation_strategy: Implement application whitelisting to prevent unauthorized software execution; enforce secure boot and driver signature enforcement; use EDR/XDR solutions with API hooking detection; deploy keyboard encryption at OS level; require multi-factor authentication to reduce credential compromise impact; conduct regular driver audits; apply principle of least privilege; monitor HID device access; use code integrity checks; maintain updated endpoint protection with behavioral analysis capabilities.

threat_vector: SQL Injection in RegistrationMagic WordPress plugin (CVE-2021-24499) via rm_chronos_ajax AJAX action during batch task duplication
exploitation_method: Attacker sends crafted AJAX request to rm_chronos_ajax action with malicious SQL syntax in user-controllable parameters used in batch task duplication queries. Unescaped input is directly concatenated into SQL statements, allowing arbitrary database queries without authentication.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: POST requests to /wp-admin/admin-ajax.php?action=rm_chronos_ajax with unusual characters (quotes, dashes, OR keywords) in task duplication parameters; WordPress database error logs showing SQL syntax errors; audit logs showing unexpected database schema modifications or data exfiltration queries; plugin activity logs showing rm_chronos_ajax calls from unauthenticated users
mitigation_strategy: Update RegistrationMagic plugin to version 5.0.1.6 or later; implement Web Application Firewall rules to filter malicious SQL patterns in AJAX requests; use prepared statements with parameterized queries; implement strict capability checks requiring user authentication for rm_chronos_ajax; disable AJAX actions if plugin is inactive; apply WordPress security hardening with limited database user permissions

threat_vector: TIBCO FTL Realm Server Authentication Bypass / Unauthorized Access
exploitation_method: Attacker exploits insufficient authentication validation in Realm Server component to bypass security controls, potentially gaining unauthorized access to message flow infrastructure without proper credentials or session validation
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unauthenticated connection attempts to Realm Server ports; (2) Malformed or missing authentication tokens in connection logs; (3) Successful connections without corresponding valid authentication records; (4) Unusual realm configuration changes without authorized API calls; (5) Error logs showing authentication bypass attempts (e.g., 'authentication check failed', 'invalid realm context'); (6) Network traffic to Realm Server ports from unexpected sources
mitigation_strategy: Implement: (1) Network segmentation restricting Realm Server access to authorized clients only; (2) Mandatory authentication enforcement with multi-factor authentication; (3) Input validation for all realm configuration parameters; (4) Rate limiting on authentication attempts; (5) Monitor and log all authentication events; (6) Upgrade TIBCO FTL to patched versions addressing CVE-2023-50359 or later; (7) Implement IDS/IPS rules for Realm Server exploitation patterns; (8) Regular security audits of realm server configurations

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1574.007: Path Interception by PATH Environment Variable
exploitation_method: Attacker places malicious executable in a directory listed in the PATH environment variable before legitimate system directories. When a user executes a command by name (e.g., 'ls', 'python'), the shell searches PATH sequentially and executes the attacker's version instead of the legitimate binary.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) unusual binaries in user-writable PATH directories (/tmp, /home, current directory); (2) file hash mismatches for common system binaries; (3) PATH environment variable modifications in process execution logs; (4) suspicious process creation with parent-child relationships indicating script execution of PATH-shadowed binaries; (5) auditd rules detecting file creation in PATH directories by non-privileged users; (6) sysmon Event ID 13 for registry modifications to PATH on Windows
mitigation_strategy: Implement PATH hardening: (1) Ensure system directories appear first in PATH and are write-protected; (2) Remove current directory (.) and user-writable directories from system-wide PATH; (3) Use absolute paths in scripts instead of relying on PATH resolution; (4) Apply principle of least privilege to restrict write access to PATH directories; (5) Deploy file integrity monitoring (Tripwire, AIDE) for system binary locations; (6) Use AppArmor/SELinux to restrict binary execution from unexpected locations; (7) Audit and validate PATH settings regularly via configuration management

threat_vector: XSS reflected attack on search parameter
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious URL with unescaped JavaScript payload in search query parameter (e.g., ?search=<script>alert('xss')</script>). When victim clicks link or is redirected, the server reflects the unvalidated input directly into HTML response, causing browser to execute injected script in victim's session context.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) HTTP requests with encoded/obfuscated script tags in query parameters (%3Cscript%3E, javascript:, onerror=); (2) Web application firewall alerts on special characters in search params; (3) Response content-type mismatch warnings; (4) Browser CSP violation reports; (5) Unusual JavaScript execution originating from user input; (6) Session tokens accessed via document.cookie in logs
mitigation_strategy: Implement output encoding: HTML-escape all user input before rendering in response (convert <, >, &, ", ' to entities). Deploy Content Security Policy (CSP) with script-src restrictions. Use parameterized templating engines that auto-escape by default. Validate input length and character whitelist for search fields. Set HttpOnly and Secure flags on session cookies. Implement input validation on server-side with regex patterns rejecting script patterns. Use modern JavaScript frameworks with built-in XSS protection (React, Vue auto-escaping).

threat_vector: Wasm3 0.5.0 out-of-bounds write in CompileBlock function
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious WebAssembly bytecode with nested loop/block structures that cause the compiler to write beyond allocated buffer boundaries during compilation, potentially overwriting heap metadata or adjacent memory structures
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) SIGSEGV/SIGABRT signals during wasm3 compilation, (2) heap corruption detection via AddressSanitizer or Valgrind, (3) abnormal memory access patterns in CompileBlock stack traces, (4) segmentation faults when loading untrusted .wasm files, (5) canary value corruption in heap allocator logs
mitigation_strategy: Update Wasm3 to patched version >0.5.0, implement strict bounds checking in memory allocators, run wasm3 in sandboxed environment with memory protection, validate WebAssembly bytecode structure before compilation (reject deeply nested blocks exceeding safe thresholds), enable runtime memory sanitizers (ASan/MSan), restrict wasm compilation to trusted sources only

threat_vector: ASUS RT-AX56U Stack-Based Buffer Overflow in httpd Parameter Validation
exploitation_method: Attacker on LAN crafts oversized HTTP request parameters to httpd service without proper length validation, causing stack buffer overflow to overwrite return addresses and execute arbitrary code with httpd process privileges
detection_pattern: Monitor for: httpd process crashes or unexpected restarts; segmentation faults in httpd logs; unusual process spawning from httpd (sh, bash); abnormal network traffic from router; stack canary violations in system logs; core dumps in /var/log; HTTP requests with exceptionally long parameter values (>1024 bytes) to router admin interface; unexpected iptables/firewall rule modifications
mitigation_strategy: Immediately apply ASUS firmware patches; implement input validation with strict length limits on all httpd parameters before processing; enable Address Space Layout Randomization (ASLR) if supported; compile httpd with stack canary protection and bounds checking; restrict LAN access to router admin interface via firewall ACLs; disable unnecessary httpd features; monitor process behavior with application whitelisting; regularly audit router access logs for suspicious requests

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Direct Network Flood (T1498.001)
exploitation_method: Attacker sends high volume of traffic (TCP/UDP packets, HTTP requests) directly to target infrastructure to exhaust bandwidth, consume server resources, or trigger connection limits, causing denial of service
detection_pattern: Sudden spike in packet rate or byte count from single/multiple sources; sustained traffic 5-10x baseline; SYN flood indicators (high SYN count without corresponding ACK completion); NetFlow/sFlow records showing volumetric anomaly; firewall/IDS alerts on connection rate thresholds; access logs showing repeated requests from specific IPs within milliseconds
mitigation_strategy: Implement rate limiting at network edge (ISP-level DDoS scrubbing, CDN); configure firewall connection limits and SYN cookies; deploy anti-DDoS appliances with traffic profiling; enable automatic blackhole routing for attack sources; use load balancing across multiple servers; implement geo-blocking if applicable; maintain sufficient bandwidth capacity with traffic shaping policies; monitor baseline metrics and alert on deviations exceeding configured thresholds

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Standard Encoding (T1132)
exploitation_method: Attacker encodes command output or data using standard encoding schemes (Base64, hex, URL encoding, etc.) to obfuscate malicious payloads, evade signature detection, and bypass content filters during command and control communications or data exfiltration
detection_pattern: Look for: Base64/hex encoded strings in process arguments or network traffic; repeated encoding/decoding operations; suspicious use of encoding utilities (certutil, base64, xxd); anomalous character patterns in network payloads (high entropy Base64 sequences); child processes spawning encoding tools; PowerShell -EncodedCommand parameters; unusual POST request bodies with encoded content
mitigation_strategy: Implement network-level DLP/IDS rules to decode and inspect suspicious payloads; restrict execution of encoding/decoding tools via AppLocker/WDAC policies; monitor PowerShell execution logs for -EncodedCommand usage; enforce outbound traffic inspection with SSL/TLS decryption; use YARA/Sigma rules to detect encoded patterns; disable unnecessary encoding utilities on endpoints; implement command line argument logging and analysis for suspicious encoding patterns

threat_vector: Dnsmasq 2.86 heap-based buffer overflow in print_mac function
exploitation_method: Attacker sends crafted DNS or DHCP packets with specially formatted MAC addresses that exceed buffer boundaries in print_mac(), triggered via log_packet or dhcp_reply code paths. Overflow corrupts heap memory, potentially achieving code execution or denial of service.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: dnsmasq process crashes or segmentation faults; unexpected memory access violations in dnsmasq logs; malformed DHCP packets with oversized or null-byte-padded MAC address fields; abnormal dnsmasq CPU/memory spikes; core dumps in /var/log; syslog entries showing dnsmasq termination; heap corruption indicators via ASAN/valgrind if debugging enabled
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade dnsmasq to patched version (2.87+); implement network segmentation to restrict DHCP/DNS traffic sources; deploy packet filtering to reject malformed DHCP packets; enable dnsmasq security options (--dhcp-hostsdir restrictions, --bind-interfaces); monitor process integrity and enable core dump analysis; consider running dnsmasq in containerized/sandboxed environment with memory protections enabled

threat_vector: PeerTube Improper Access Control - Unauthorized Video/Channel Access
exploitation_method: Attacker bypasses authentication/authorization checks by manipulating API parameters, session tokens, or direct object references to access private videos, channels, or admin functions they lack permission for. May involve parameter tampering in REST endpoints, JWT token manipulation, or exploiting missing permission validation on protected resources.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: 401/403 HTTP errors followed by successful requests to restricted endpoints; API calls with modified object IDs or user parameters inconsistent with authenticated user; repeated access attempts to private video UUIDs; admin endpoints accessed from non-admin accounts; unexpected changes to video privacy settings or channel permissions in audit logs; suspicious authorization header modifications in web server logs.
mitigation_strategy: Implement strict role-based access control (RBAC) with server-side authorization checks on all endpoints before returning resources; validate user permissions on every API request using authenticated session data, not client-supplied identifiers; enforce principle of least privilege; conduct regular authorization testing; use security headers (X-Frame-Options, Content-Security-Policy); implement audit logging for permission changes; keep PeerTube patched to latest version; conduct code review of access control logic; use allow-lists rather than deny-lists for permissions.

threat_vector: Foxit PDF Reader/Editor pre-11.1 macOS XFA API Remote Code Execution
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious PDF containing XFA (XML Forms Architecture) script that invokes xfa.host.gotoURL with arbitrary command execution payload. When victim opens PDF in vulnerable Foxit version, XFA script executes with application privileges, allowing arbitrary code execution on the system.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Foxit process spawning unexpected child processes with suspicious arguments; (2) Network connections initiated by Foxit to non-standard ports or suspicious domains; (3) File system modifications in /tmp, ~/Library/Caches, or user home directories immediately after PDF opening; (4) Unusual process trees with Foxit as parent; (5) PDF file uploads containing <xfa:> XML tags with gotoURL methods; (6) Foxit crash logs or error messages related to XFA script execution
mitigation_strategy: Immediate: (1) Update Foxit PDF Reader/Editor to version 11.1 or later on all macOS systems; (2) Implement application whitelisting to restrict Foxit child process execution; (3) Disable XFA script execution in Foxit preferences if available; (4) Block untrusted PDF sources at email gateway and web proxy; (5) Enforce sandboxing via macOS System Integrity Protection; (6) Monitor Foxit process behavior with EDR tools; (7) Educate users against opening PDFs from untrusted sources; (8) Consider restricting Foxit to read-only mode for high-risk users

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Rootkit (T1014)
exploitation_method: Attacker loads kernel-mode or user-mode code to gain persistent privileged access, hiding processes, files, network connections, and system activity from detection tools by intercepting system calls and manipulating OS data structures
detection_pattern: Kernel integrity violations, unsigned drivers loading, discrepancies between syscall results and raw memory inspection, hidden process detection via cross-referenced PID enumeration, anomalous system calls with hooked addresses, elevated CPU/memory from hidden processes, filesystem inconsistencies between different enumeration methods
mitigation_strategy: Enable Secure Boot and UEFI firmware security, enforce driver signing requirements and block unsigned kernel loads, deploy kernel patch protection (KPP/HyperGuard), use memory integrity (HVCI) and virtualization-based security, implement rootkit detection tools (GMER, Volatility), maintain integrity monitoring of critical system binaries, apply EDR solutions with kernel-level monitoring, keep OS patches current, disable unnecessary kernel extensions, use read-only boot partitions where feasible

threat_vector: SQL Injection in Zoho ManageEngine Applications Manager showReports module (pre-build 14550)
exploitation_method: Authenticated attacker crafts malicious SQL queries embedded in request parameters to the showReports module, bypassing input validation to extract, modify, or delete database records without authorization
detection_pattern: Monitor for: SQL syntax keywords (UNION, SELECT, WHERE, DROP, INSERT, UPDATE) in showReports request parameters; unusual database query patterns; multiple failed database queries followed by successful ones; error responses containing SQL exception details; access logs showing authenticated users making requests with encoded SQL payloads; database audit logs showing unexpected query execution from application service accounts
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade Zoho ManageEngine Applications Manager to build 14550 or later; implement parameterized queries and prepared statements for all database interactions in showReports module; enforce strict input validation and whitelist allowed characters for all user inputs; apply principle of least privilege to database service account credentials; enable SQL query logging and anomaly detection; conduct code review of showReports module for SQL injection vulnerabilities; implement Web Application Firewall rules to detect and block SQL injection patterns

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Pluggable Authentication Modules (PAM) Manipulation
exploitation_method: Attacker modifies PAM configuration files (/etc/pam.d/*) or injects malicious PAM modules to intercept authentication credentials, bypass authentication checks, or establish persistence by creating backdoor authentication paths that execute arbitrary code during login processes.
detection_pattern: Monitor for unauthorized modifications to /etc/pam.d/ directory and files, unexpected PAM module loads from non-standard library paths, unusual process execution during authentication (auditd rules for PAM-related syscalls), unexpected library dependencies in pam_*.so files, file integrity monitoring alerts on authentication-critical files, and authentication logs showing successful logins with disabled/modified PAM modules.
mitigation_strategy: Implement strict file integrity monitoring (AIDE, Tripwire) on /etc/pam.d/ and PAM libraries; restrict write permissions to PAM configuration files (644 max for configs, 755 for directories); deploy SELinux or AppArmor policies restricting PAM module loading; maintain audit logging of all pam.d modifications; regularly verify PAM configuration integrity against baseline; implement code signing verification for PAM modules; restrict root-level access and implement privileged access management (PAM); disable unused PAM modules; keep PAM packages updated.

threat_vector: Atlassian Jira Service Management Server/Data Center - Authenticated Information Disclosure via Object Import Configuration
exploitation_method: Authenticated attacker accesses object import configuration endpoints without proper authorization checks, retrieving sensitive configuration details including credentials, API keys, database connection strings, or integration parameters stored in import settings.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: HTTP requests to /secure/admin/ObjectImporter*.jspa or similar configuration endpoints from non-admin authenticated users; access logs showing repeated configuration retrieval attempts; audit logs showing unauthorized access to ObjectImporterConfiguration or related admin pages; unusual API calls to configuration management endpoints by low-privilege accounts.
mitigation_strategy: 1) Upgrade Atlassian Jira Service Management to patched version; 2) Implement role-based access control (RBAC) restricting object import configuration access to system administrators only; 3) Audit and rotate any credentials previously exposed in configuration; 4) Enable request logging and monitor for unauthorized configuration access attempts; 5) Apply principle of least privilege to user accounts; 6) Review audit logs for historical unauthorized access.

threat_vector: Integer overflow in storeAtts function of xmlparse.c in Expat library versions before 2.4.3
exploitation_method: Attacker sends specially crafted XML documents with excessive attributes to trigger integer overflow in attribute storage calculation, causing memory corruption, buffer overflow, or heap overflow that can lead to code execution or denial of service
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) XML parsing crashes or segmentation faults when processing documents with unusually large numbers of attributes; (2) Memory sanitizer alerts (ASan/MSan) indicating heap buffer overflow near attribute processing; (3) Library version detection showing libexpat < 2.4.3 in use; (4) Unexpected termination of XML parsing services with core dumps; (5) Unusual memory allocation patterns during XML document ingestion
mitigation_strategy: Immediately upgrade Expat library to version 2.4.3 or later; implement input validation to limit maximum number of attributes per element; deploy memory protection mechanisms (ASLR, DEP/NX); run XML parsers in sandboxed environments with restricted privileges; monitor and limit resource consumption during XML parsing; implement strict bounds checking on attribute counts before processing

threat_vector: Stack-based buffer overflow in SonicOS HTTP Content-Length response header processing
exploitation_method: Attacker sends HTTP response with maliciously crafted Content-Length header value that exceeds stack buffer boundaries. When SonicOS parses this header without proper bounds checking, the overflow corrupts stack memory, potentially overwriting return addresses or control flow data.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: HTTP response packets with Content-Length values exceeding typical ranges (>2GB); application crashes or segmentation faults in SonicOS HTTP parsing processes; unexpected stack corruption errors in debug logs; repeated connection resets from same source after HTTP responses; anomalous memory access violations in HTTP handler processes; IDS signatures detecting oversized Content-Length fields in responses
mitigation_strategy: Apply latest SonicWall security patches immediately; implement strict input validation with maximum bounds checking on Content-Length headers (reject values >2GB); deploy WAF rules to filter malformed HTTP responses; enable stack canary protection and ASLR on SonicOS; restrict HTTP response sources through firewall rules; monitor and alert on HTTP parser exceptions; implement network segmentation to limit exposure of administrative interfaces; maintain disabled unnecessary HTTP response processing where possible

threat_vector: wolfSSL wolfMQTT 1.9 heap-based buffer overflow in MqttDecode_Publish
exploitation_method: Attacker sends a crafted MQTT PUBLISH packet with a maliciously constructed payload that exceeds allocated buffer boundaries by 8 bytes when processed by MqttDecode_Publish function, potentially overwriting adjacent heap memory structures
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) MQTT PUBLISH packets with payload length fields inconsistent with actual packet size; (2) Process crashes or abnormal termination of MQTT clients after receiving specific packets; (3) Address Sanitizer (ASan) reports of heap-buffer-overflow at MqttDecode_Publish; (4) Unexpected memory corruption detected through heap integrity checks; (5) Application segmentation faults with stack traces pointing to wolfMQTT decode functions
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade wolfSSL wolfMQTT library to version 1.10 or later where buffer overflow is patched; implement input validation for PUBLISH packet payload lengths before processing; deploy runtime memory protection (ASan, Valgrind) in development/testing; use memory-safe configurations and enable heap canaries; implement packet size limits and rate limiting on MQTT broker; apply network segmentation to restrict MQTT traffic to trusted clients only

threat_vector: Peripheral Device Discovery (T1120)
exploitation_method: Attacker executes commands (e.g., Get-PnpDevice, lsusb, ioreg) or queries Windows Registry/sysfs to enumerate connected USB devices, printers, storage media, and other peripherals to identify targets for lateral movement, data exfiltration, or supply chain compromise
detection_pattern: Monitor for: WMI queries (Win32_PnPEntity, Win32_USBControllerDevice), PowerShell Get-PnpDevice execution, repeated ioreg/system_profiler calls on macOS, lsusb/lspci execution on Linux, Registry access to HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Enum, usbmon kernel module activity, and unusual permission checks on /dev/bus/usb entries
mitigation_strategy: Restrict execution of enumeration tools via AppLocker/WDAC policies; disable unnecessary WMI classes; disable PowerShell execution policies; monitor and alert on unexpected device discovery activity; segment USB ports with physical restrictions or software USB filtering; implement device whitelisting policies; educate users about untrusted peripheral risks

threat_vector: DOM-based XSS in Scratch-Svg-Renderer v0.2.0 via malicious sb3 file
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts a malicious .sb3 file containing JavaScript or HTML payloads in SVG elements or metadata that execute in the DOM when the renderer parses and displays the file without proper sanitization
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unexpected script tag execution in browser console during sb3 file rendering; (2) DOM mutations inserting <script> or event handlers (onclick, onerror, onload); (3) Network requests from unexpected origins following sb3 file import; (4) WAF/XSS filter logs showing script injection attempts in file uploads; (5) Content Security Policy (CSP) violations in browser developer tools
mitigation_strategy: Implement comprehensive input validation: (1) Update Scratch-Svg-Renderer to v0.2.1+ with XSS fixes; (2) Sanitize all SVG content using DOMPurify or similar library before DOM insertion; (3) Enforce strict Content Security Policy (no-inline scripts, script-src whitelist); (4) Validate sb3 file structure against schema before processing; (5) Disable dangerous SVG features (script elements, event handlers); (6) Use iframe sandbox for untrusted renderers; (7) Implement output encoding for any user-controlled data displayed in DOM

threat_vector: libde265 v1.0.8 assertion failure in sps.cc:925 via malformed HEVC file
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious HEVC-encoded video file with invalid scaling_list_pred_matrix_id_delta value that triggers assertion check at line 925 of sps.cc during sequence parameter set parsing, causing application crash
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Application crash with SIGABRT signal during video decoding, (2) Core dump indicating assertion failure at libde265:sps.cc:925, (3) Excessive memory access patterns to scaling list structures, (4) Failed decoding attempts with specific HEVC files returning parse errors, (5) Process termination logs referencing scaling_list_pred_matrix_id validation
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade libde265 to patched version (>1.0.8) with fixed assertion validation; implement input validation to reject HEVC files with invalid scaling_list_pred_matrix_id_delta values before processing; deploy exception handling wrapper around decoder to catch crashes gracefully; run decoder in sandboxed process with resource limits to isolate DoS impact; use file type validation and HEVC format verification before decoding

threat_vector: Unvalidated PUSCH (Physical Uplink Shared Channel) configuration parameters in Snapdragon chipsets causing assertion failures and denial of service
exploitation_method: Attacker sends malformed or out-of-range PUSCH configuration messages (frequency allocation, resource block assignments, modulation parameters) that bypass input validation, triggering assertion failures in modem firmware and crashing the cellular stack
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unexpected modem crashes with assertion logs containing PUSCH-related stack traces, repeated cellular connectivity loss without radio error codes, kernel panic logs referencing modem heap corruption, anomalous PUSCH parameter values in radio diagnostic logs, unexpected firmware trap handlers triggered during normal operation
mitigation_strategy: Implement strict input validation for all PUSCH configuration parameters with range checks before processing; add assert-to-error-handling conversion in modem firmware to gracefully reject invalid configs; enable modem firmware integrity verification and secure boot; deploy network-side message validation in base stations; implement rate limiting on configuration change requests; update to latest security patches that address PUSCH parameter validation gaps

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Windows Host Firewall (T1562.004) - Disabling or Modifying Windows Defender Firewall
exploitation_method: Attacker uses elevated privileges to disable Windows Firewall via netsh commands, PowerShell Set-NetFirewallProfile cmdlets, or direct registry modification of HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\SharedAccess\Parameters\FirewallPolicy. Can also delete firewall rules to allow unauthorized traffic.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: netsh advfirewall set allprofiles state off commands in process execution logs; PowerShell execution of Set-NetFirewallProfile -Enabled False; Registry modifications to FirewallPolicy keys; WMI Win32_FirewallProduct state changes; Event ID 2004 (firewall state changed) in Windows Logs/System; Unexpected removal of inbound/outbound rules via Event ID 2005
mitigation_strategy: Implement Group Policy restrictions via gpedit.msc to prevent firewall modifications (Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Windows Firewall with Advanced Security); Use AppLocker/WDAC to block unauthorized netsh.exe and powershell.exe execution; Enable audit logging for firewall changes; Deploy EDR solutions to detect real-time firewall state manipulation; Restrict local admin privileges; Use Windows Advanced Threat Protection for behavioral analysis; Enforce signed PowerShell scripts only

threat_vector: ATT&CK: Databases (T1005)
exploitation_method: Attacker gains unauthorized access to database systems through compromised credentials, SQL injection, unpatched vulnerabilities, or misconfigured access controls. Once authenticated, they enumerate schema, extract sensitive data, or modify records for lateral movement and data exfiltration.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: unusual database query patterns (SELECT * queries, multiple failed authentication attempts), abnormal database user activity outside business hours, bulk data export operations, privilege escalation attempts in audit logs, unexpected schema changes, connections from non-standard ports/IPs, and correlation of database logs with endpoint detection alerts on database service accounts
mitigation_strategy: Implement principle of least privilege with role-based access control (RBAC), enforce strong password policies and MFA for database accounts, apply WAF/input validation to prevent SQL injection, patch database systems regularly, encrypt data at rest and in transit, use database activity monitoring (DAM) solutions, implement network segmentation to restrict database access, maintain comprehensive audit logging with immutable storage, and conduct regular database security assessments

threat_vector: Heap overflow vulnerability in Bone voice ID threat actor infrastructure
exploitation_method: Attacker sends specially crafted input exceeding heap buffer boundaries in voice ID processing component, overwriting adjacent heap memory structures and injecting malicious code or redirecting execution flow to attacker-controlled payload
detection_pattern: Monitor for: abnormal process termination with segmentation faults, unexpected child process spawning from voice service, heap corruption indicators in memory dumps, unusual system calls following voice processing requests, stack traces showing heap corruption errors in logs, memory access violations at unexpected addresses, or voice input samples with abnormal byte patterns/sizes exceeding documented limits
mitigation_strategy: Implement heap-based exploit protections: enable Address Space Layout Randomization (ASLR), stack canaries, and Control Flow Guard (CFG); deploy input validation with strict size limits on voice samples; use memory-safe libraries for audio processing; apply available security patches immediately; implement runtime monitoring with Valgrind or AddressSanitizer in development; segment voice ID service with reduced privilege levels; apply Data Execution Prevention (DEP); conduct regular fuzzing of voice input handling code

threat_vector: ATT&CK T1530: Data from Cloud Storage
exploitation_method: Attacker gains unauthorized access to cloud storage buckets (S3, Azure Blob, GCS) through misconfigured permissions, leaked credentials, or compromised accounts, then exfiltrates sensitive data stored in these repositories
detection_pattern: Unusual bulk download operations from cloud storage; access from unfamiliar IP addresses or geolocations; multiple failed authentication attempts followed by successful access; API calls listing or downloading large numbers of objects; access patterns deviating from baseline user behavior; CloudTrail/audit logs showing GetObject operations on sensitive buckets outside business hours
mitigation_strategy: Implement least-privilege IAM policies with explicit bucket/container access controls; enable versioning and MFA delete protection; configure bucket encryption at rest; deploy cloud access security brokers (CASB) to monitor anomalous data access; enable detailed audit logging and alerts for bulk downloads; regularly audit and restrict public/anonymous access; implement network segmentation and egress filtering; enforce multi-factor authentication for cloud account access; conduct periodic cloud storage permission audits

threat_vector: Unauthenticated Named Pipe channel in Controlup Real-Time Agent (cuAgent.exe) before 8.5 allows arbitrary OS command execution via ProcessActionRequest WCF method
exploitation_method: Attacker connects to the named pipe endpoint without authentication, crafts a malicious WCF message containing OS commands in the ProcessActionRequest method parameters, and executes arbitrary code with the privileges of the cuAgent.exe process
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Unexpected connections to named pipe 'ControlUp' or similar pipes from non-privileged accounts; (2) WCF method calls to ProcessActionRequest with suspicious command payloads in event logs; (3) cuAgent.exe spawning child processes with unusual command lines or from unexpected parent-child relationships; (4) Network traffic to named pipe endpoints from localhost with encoding anomalies; (5) Process execution audit logs showing commands executed by cuAgent service account that weren't initiated by legitimate Controlup management console
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade Controlup Real-Time Agent to version 8.5 or later; implement named pipe access controls restricting connections to authenticated users only; run cuAgent.exe with minimal required privileges; deploy application whitelisting to restrict child process execution from cuAgent; implement WCF binding security requiring message-level authentication and integrity checking; monitor and restrict network access to named pipe endpoints; enable Windows Event Forwarding for process creation and WCF activity logs

threat_vector: Missing input validation before memory copy in TIMA trustlet (pre-SMR Jan-2022 Release 1)
exploitation_method: Attacker sends crafted input to TIMA trustlet without proper bounds checking, causing memcpy or similar operation to read from arbitrary memory addresses outside intended buffers, enabling information disclosure from sensitive kernel or TEE memory regions
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) TIMA trustlet processing unexpected buffer sizes exceeding defined limits, (2) memory access patterns reading from addresses outside allocated ranges, (3) trustlet returning data corresponding to kernel memory regions, (4) fuzzing inputs with varying sizes to trustlet interfaces, (5) audit logs showing copy operations with mismatched source/destination bounds
mitigation_strategy: Apply TIMA SMR Jan-2022 Release 1 or later security update; implement strict input validation checking buffer sizes against maximum allowed values before all copy operations; use safe memory functions with explicit length parameters; enable address space layout randomization (ASLR) and memory tagging extensions (MTE) in TEE; conduct security testing with boundary value analysis on all trustlet input handlers

threat_vector: Lua v5.4.3+ SEGV via type confusion in funcnamefromcode (ldebug.c)
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malformed debug information or Lua bytecode that triggers type confusion in the funcnamefromcode function during stack unwinding or debug introspection, causing a segmentation fault and process crash
detection_pattern: Monitor for: SIGSEGV signals in Lua processes; core dumps with funcnamefromcode in stack trace; unexpected Lua interpreter crashes when processing debug.getinfo() or stack trace operations; abnormal termination with signal 11; repeated process crashes correlating with specific Lua script execution
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade Lua to version 5.4.4 or later which contains the type confusion fix; implement process sandboxing with resource limits to contain crashes; disable debug library access in untrusted code paths using Lua environment restrictions; implement watchdog timers and automatic process restart mechanisms for long-running Lua interpreters; validate all bytecode sources before execution

threat_vector: Open Asset Import Library (assimp) 5.1.0-5.1.1 heap-based buffer overflow in _m3d_safestr function
exploitation_method: Attacker crafts malicious 3D model file (M3D format) with specially crafted string data that exceeds buffer boundaries in the _m3d_safestr function. When assimp parses the file via m3d_load or M3DWrapper constructor, the unsafe string handling causes heap memory corruption, potentially enabling code execution.
detection_pattern: Monitor for: (1) Heap corruption signals (ASAN/valgrind heap-buffer-overflow reports), (2) Abnormal process termination with segmentation faults when loading M3D files, (3) Unexpected memory access patterns to heap regions during model parsing, (4) Application crashes in assimp library stack frames containing '_m3d_safestr', (5) File upload events involving .m3d files with unusually large string fields or malformed headers
mitigation_strategy: Upgrade assimp to version 5.1.2 or later where the buffer overflow is patched.
